== Onsen Town == [[File:Manza ski resort Mt.Asahi 20080219.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Manza Onsen Ski Resort|ja|万座温泉スキー場}}]] Located on the slopes of {{ill|Kusatsu Shirane|ja|草津白根山}}, Manza Onsen was once a thriving hot spring resort ({{ill|Yūji|ja|湯治場}}) from spring through autumn and is now renowned as a ski destination in winter. The high temperature of its sources causes a continuous plume of steam, and the source areas display a hellish appearance with a pervasive sulfur odor. Hot spring sources emerge abundantly along the riverbed<ref name="noguchi">[[ill|Fuyuhito Noguchi|ja|野口冬人]]『湯治の宿』, Yomiuri Travel Publishing</ref>. A notable vent known as "Karabuki" near the outskirts of the central area is a local specialty (though its steam output has diminished since around 2012). The gently sloping mountainside is dotted with inns and hotels, with the {{ill|Manza Onsen Ski Resort|ja|万座温泉スキー場}} situated at the rear. Because the area is within a national park, facility expansions or renovations are limited, resulting in many aging structures. Some accommodations have closed or been absorbed by others; as of 2019, only 7 inns and hotels continue independent operations for individual guests. There are also lodging options with self-catering facilities for long-term hot spring therapy, though no convenience stores exist in the area apart from small shops attached to the accommodations. Additionally, the Manza Onsen Ski Resort was featured as a filming location for the movie [[Take Me Skiing]]. There are over 10 hot spring sources, including Yubatake, Banna no Yu, Daiku Yu, Tetsuyu, and Hōsei no Yu.
== History == The period of initial development is unclear; however, Yayoi pottery has been excavated in the vicinity. Legend has it that {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂}} exorcised demons here, and records indicate that samurai visited for hot spring therapy during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}. Although it operated as a hot spring resort during the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, it did not flourish until after World War II, when the {{ill|Seibu Group|ja|西武グループ}} undertook resort development and transportation improved.
On February 22, 1983, the old building of Manza Onsen Hotel (Nisshinkan) caught fire, completely burning an area of 1,000 m². Four ski guests sustained minor injuries. According to the reports of the {{ill|Naganohara Police Station|ja|長野原警察署}} and the {{ill|Agatsuma Western Regional Fire Department|ja|吾妻広域西部消防署}}, although 135 guests were staying in the old building, the deep snow enabled most to escape by jumping from the windows, preventing a far worse tragedy<ref name="mainichi">[[ill|Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞]] Evening Edition, February 23, 1983</ref>.
In 2004, during the hot spring misrepresentation scandal, a ryokan was reported to have displayed descriptions on its website suggesting hot spring usage despite not drawing any; subsequent corrections were made. As of 2019, the management of the affected facility has changed.
== Access == * Railway ({{ill|JR East|ja|JR東日本}}) : Approximately 40 minutes by {{ill|Seibu Sightseeing Bus|ja|西武観光バス}} from {{ill|Manza-Kashizawa-guchi Station|ja|万座・鹿沢口駅}} on the {{ill|Agatsuma Line|ja|吾妻線}}. : Approximately 90 minutes by Seibu Sightseeing Bus (via Manza-Kashizawa-guchi Station) from {{ill|Karuizawa Station|ja|軽井沢駅}} on the {{ill|Hokuriku Shinkansen|ja|北陸新幹線}}. * There are three bus stops in the Manza Onsen area: "Manza Onsen" (near the entrance), the central "Manza Prince Hotel" bus stop, and the "Manza Onsen Ue" bus stop. * Automobile: : The only road available year‑round is the paid {{ill|Manza Highway|ja|万座ハイウェー}}, about 22 km from Mihara in Tsumagoi Village. : From spring to autumn, {{ill|National Route 292|ja|国道292号}} (Shiga‑Kusatsu Kogen Route) is available (it is closed in winter). Note that due to the eruption alert level for {{ill|Kusatsu Shirane|ja|草津白根山}}, the section between the (Kusatsu) {{ill|Sesshōgahara|ja|殺生河原}} parking area and the Manza Sansaji intersection may be closed. As of April 2019, the eruption alert level is 2; although normally it would be closed all day, an exception allows passage during daytime only (bicycles, motorcycles, and convertibles are not permitted; weather conditions such as fog may also cause closures).
== Footnotes ==
<references />
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Manza Onsen Ski Resort|ja|万座温泉スキー場}} * {{ill|Manza Prince Hotel|ja|万座プリンスホテル}} * {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉}} * {{ill|ii Yudana|ja|いい湯だな}} ({{ill|Duke Aces|ja|デューク・エイセス}}) – Third set of lyrics
== External Links == * [http://www.manzaonsen.gr.jp/ Manza Onsen Tourism Association]
{{Normdaten}}

[[File:Kusatsu-Shiranesan01s5s4272.jpg|thumb|image]] '''Manza Onsen''' (万座温泉) is a hot spring located in Hoshimata, {{ill|Tsumagoi Village|ja|嬬恋村|12=draft}}, {{ill|Agatsuma District|ja|吾妻郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|群馬県|12=draft}}, within the {{ill|Joshinetsu Kogen National Park|ja|上信越高原国立公園|12=draft}}, at an elevation of 1,800 m. It is one of the highest hot springs in Japan that can be reached year‑round by private automobile, along with {{ill|Daku River Onsen|ja|濁河温泉|12=draft}} in {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県|12=draft}}’s {{ill|Gero City|ja|下呂市|12=draft}}. Nearby are three explosive crater lakes: Yubatake, Karagama, and Mizukama.
== Water Quality == * Acidic sulfur spring ({{ill|Hydrogen Sulfide Spring|ja|硫化水素泉|12=draft}}) : The water is characterized by its milky white or yellowish appearance and contains the highest sulfur content of any hot spring in Japan. It has a discharge of 3,750 L per minute.
== Onsen Town == [[File:Manza ski resort Mt.Asahi 20080219.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Manza Onsen Ski Resort|ja|万座温泉スキー場|12=draft}}]] Located on the slopes of {{ill|Kusatsu Shirane|ja|草津白根山|12=draft}}, Manza Onsen was once a thriving hot spring resort ({{ill|Yūji|ja|湯治場|12=draft}}) from spring through autumn and is now renowned as a ski destination in winter. The gently sloping mountainside is dotted with inns and hotels, with the {{ill|Manza Onsen Ski Resort|ja|万座温泉スキー場|12=draft}} situated at the rear. Because the area is within a national park, facility expansions or renovations are limited, resulting in many aging structures. Some accommodations have closed or been absorbed by others; as of 2019, only 7 inns and hotels continue independent operations for individual guests. There are also lodging options with self-catering facilities for long-term hot spring therapy, though no convenience stores exist in the area apart from small shops attached to the accommodations. Additionally, the Manza Onsen Ski Resort was featured as a filming location for the movie [[Take Me Skiing]]. There are over 10 hot spring sources, including Yubatake, Banna no Yu, Daiku Yu, Tetsuyu, and Hōsei no Yu.

Legend has it that {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂|12=draft}} exorcised demons here, and records indicate that samurai visited for hot spring therapy during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=draft}}. Although it operated as a hot spring resort during the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, it did not flourish until after World War II, when the {{ill|Seibu Group|ja|西武グループ|12=draft}} undertook resort development and transportation improved.
On February 22, 1983, the old building of Manza Onsen Hotel (Nisshinkan) caught fire, completely burning an area of 1,000 m². Four ski guests sustained minor injuries. According to the reports of the {{ill|Naganohara Police Station|ja|長野原警察署|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Agatsuma Western Regional Fire Department|ja|吾妻広域西部消防署|12=draft}}, although 135 guests were staying in the old building, the deep snow enabled most to escape by jumping from the windows, preventing a far worse tragedy<ref name="mainichi">[[ill|Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞]] Evening Edition, February 23, 1983</ref>.
In 2004, during the hot spring misrepresentation scandal, a ryokan was reported to have displayed descriptions on its website suggesting hot spring usage despite not drawing any; subsequent corrections were made. As of 2019, the management of the affected facility has changed.
== Access == * Railway ({{ill|JR East|ja|JR東日本|12=draft}}) : Approximately 40 minutes by {{ill|Seibu Sightseeing Bus|ja|西武観光バス|12=draft}} from {{ill|Manza-Kashizawa-guchi Station|ja|万座・鹿沢口駅|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Agatsuma Line|ja|吾妻線|12=draft}}. : Approximately 90 minutes by Seibu Sightseeing Bus (via Manza-Kashizawa-guchi Station) from {{ill|Karuizawa Station|ja|軽井沢駅|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Hokuriku Shinkansen|ja|北陸新幹線|12=draft}}. * There are three bus stops in the Manza Onsen area: "Manza Onsen" (near the entrance), the central "Manza Prince Hotel" bus stop, and the "Manza Onsen Ue" bus stop. * Automobile: : The only road available year‑round is the paid {{ill|Manza Highway|ja|万座ハイウェー|12=draft}}, about 22 km from Mihara in Tsumagoi Village. : From spring to autumn, {{ill|National Route 292|ja|国道292号|12=draft}} (Shiga‑Kusatsu Kogen Route) is available (it is closed in winter). Legend has it that {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂|12=draft}} exorcised demons here, and records indicate that samurai visited for hot spring therapy during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=draft}}. Although it operated as a hot spring resort during the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, it did not flourish until after World War II, when the {{ill|Seibu Group|ja|西武グループ|12=draft}} undertook resort development and transportation improved.
On February 22, 1983, the old building of Manza Onsen Hotel (Nisshinkan) caught fire, completely burning an area of 1,000 m². Four ski guests sustained minor injuries. According to the reports of the {{ill|Naganohara Police Station|ja|長野原警察署|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Agatsuma Western Regional Fire Department|ja|吾妻広域西部消防署|12=draft}}, although 135 guests were staying in the old building, the deep snow enabled most to escape by jumping from the windows, preventing a far worse tragedy<ref name="mainichi">[[ill|Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞]] Evening Edition, February 23, 1983</ref>.
In 2004, during the hot spring misrepresentation scandal, a ryokan was reported to have displayed descriptions on its website suggesting hot spring usage despite not drawing any; subsequent corrections were made. As of 2019, the management of the affected facility has changed.
== Access == * Railway ({{ill|JR East|ja|JR東日本|12=draft}}) : Approximately 40 minutes by {{ill|Seibu Sightseeing Bus|ja|西武観光バス|12=draft}} from {{ill|Manza-Kashizawa-guchi Station|ja|万座・鹿沢口駅|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Agatsuma Line|ja|吾妻線|12=draft}}. : Approximately 90 minutes by Seibu Sightseeing Bus (via Manza-Kashizawa-guchi Station) from {{ill|Karuizawa Station|ja|軽井沢駅|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Hokuriku Shinkansen|ja|北陸新幹線|12=draft}}. * There are three bus stops in the Manza Onsen area: "Manza Onsen" (near the entrance), the central "Manza Prince Hotel" bus stop, and the "Manza Onsen Ue" bus stop. * Automobile: : The only road available year‑round is the paid {{ill|Manza Highway|ja|万座ハイウェー|12=draft}}, about 22 km from Mihara in Tsumagoi Village. : From spring to autumn, {{ill|National Route 292|ja|国道292号|12=draft}} (Shiga‑Kusatsu Kogen Route) is available (it is closed in winter). Note that due to the eruption alert level for {{ill|Kusatsu Shirane|ja|草津白根山|12=draft}}, the section between the (Kusatsu) {{ill|Sesshōgahara|ja|殺生河原|12=draft}} parking area and the Manza Sansaji intersection may be closed. As of April 2019, the eruption alert level is 2; although normally it would be closed all day, an exception allows passage during daytime only (bicycles, motorcycles, and convertibles are not permitted; weather conditions such as fog may also cause closures).

{{出典の明記|date=March 2017}}{{infobox Onsen | 名称 = Manza Onsen | native_name = 万座温泉 | image = Manza Onsen Tsumagoi Gunma01bs4272.jpg | コメント = Manza-yama and Manza Onsen | 所在地 = {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|群馬県|12=draft}} {{ill|Agatsuma District|ja|吾妻郡|12=draft}} {{ill|Tsumagoi Village|ja|嬬恋村|12=draft}} 干俣 | 緯度度 = 36 | 緯度分 = 38 | 緯度秒 = 5.1 | 経度度 = 138 | 経度分 = 30 | 経度秒 = 31.7 | 交通アクセス = ''See the [[#Access|Access]] section.'' | 泉質 = Acidic sulfur spring | 泉温 = Approximately 80°C | 湧出量 = Approximately 5,400,000 {{ill|L|ja|リットル|12=draft}}/day | pH = | 液性の分類 = | 浸透圧の分類 = | 宿泊施設数 = 9 | 総収容人員数 = | 年間浴客数 = | 統計年度 = | 外部リンク = http://www.manzaonsen.gr.jp/ | 特記事項 = }}
[[File:Kusatsu-Shiranesan01s5s4272.jpg|thumb|image]] '''Manza Onsen''' (万座温泉) is a hot spring located in Hoshimata, {{ill|Tsumagoi Village|ja|嬬恋村|12=draft}}, {{ill|Agatsuma District|ja|吾妻郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|群馬県|12=draft}}, within the {{ill|Joshinetsu Kogen National Park|ja|上信越高原国立公園|12=draft}}, at an elevation of 1,800 m. It is one of the highest hot springs in Japan that can be reached year‑round by private automobile, along with {{ill|Daku River Onsen|ja|濁河温泉|12=draft}} in {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県|12=draft}}’s {{ill|Gero City|ja|下呂市|12=draft}}. Nearby are three explosive crater lakes: Yubatake, Karagama, and Mizukama.
== Water Quality == * Acidic sulfur spring ({{ill|Hydrogen Sulfide Spring|ja|硫化水素泉|12=draft}}) : The water is characterized by its milky white or yellowish appearance and contains the highest sulfur content of any hot spring in Japan. It has a discharge of 3,750 L per minute.
== Onsen Town == [[File:Manza ski resort Mt.Asahi 20080219.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Manza Onsen Ski Resort|ja|万座温泉スキー場|12=draft}}]] Located on the slopes of {{ill|Kusatsu Shirane|ja|草津白根山|12=draft}}, Manza Onsen was once a thriving hot spring resort ({{ill|Yūji|ja|湯治場|12=draft}}) from spring through autumn and is now renowned as a ski destination in winter. The high temperature of its sources causes a continuous plume of steam, and the source areas display a hellish appearance with a pervasive sulfur odor. Hot spring sources emerge abundantly along the riverbed<ref name="noguchi">[[ill|Fuyuhito Noguchi|ja|野口冬人]]『湯治の宿』, Yomiuri Travel Publishing</ref>. A notable vent known as "Karabuki" near the outskirts of the central area is a local specialty (though its steam output has diminished since around 2012). The gently sloping mountainside is dotted with inns and hotels, with the {{ill|Manza Onsen Ski Resort|ja|万座温泉スキー場|12=draft}} situated at the rear. Because the area is within a national park, facility expansions or renovations are limited, resulting in many aging structures. Some accommodations have closed or been absorbed by others; as of 2019, only 7 inns and hotels continue independent operations for individual guests. There are also lodging options with self-catering facilities for long-term hot spring therapy, though no convenience stores exist in the area apart from small shops attached to the accommodations. Additionally, the Manza Onsen Ski Resort was featured as a filming location for the movie [[Take Me Skiing]]. There are over 10 hot spring sources, including Yubatake, Banna no Yu, Daiku Yu, Tetsuyu, and Hōsei no Yu.
== History == The period of initial development is unclear; however, Yayoi pottery has been excavated in the vicinity. Legend has it that {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂|12=draft}} exorcised demons here, and records indicate that samurai visited for hot spring therapy during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=draft}}. Although it operated as a hot spring resort during the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, it did not flourish until after World War II, when the {{ill|Seibu Group|ja|西武グループ|12=draft}} undertook resort development and transportation improved.
On February 22, 1983, the old building of Manza Onsen Hotel (Nisshinkan) caught fire, completely burning an area of 1,000 m². Four ski guests sustained minor injuries. According to the reports of the {{ill|Naganohara Police Station|ja|長野原警察署|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Agatsuma Western Regional Fire Department|ja|吾妻広域西部消防署|12=draft}}, although 135 guests were staying in the old building, the deep snow enabled most to escape by jumping from the windows, preventing a far worse tragedy<ref name="mainichi">[[ill|Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞]] Evening Edition, February 23, 1983</ref>.
In 2004, during the hot spring misrepresentation scandal, a ryokan was reported to have displayed descriptions on its website suggesting hot spring usage despite not drawing any; subsequent corrections were made. As of 2019, the management of the affected facility has changed.
== Access == * Railway ({{ill|JR East|ja|JR東日本|12=draft}}) : Approximately 40 minutes by {{ill|Seibu Sightseeing Bus|ja|西武観光バス|12=draft}} from {{ill|Manza-Kashizawa-guchi Station|ja|万座・鹿沢口駅|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Agatsuma Line|ja|吾妻線|12=draft}}. : Approximately 90 minutes by Seibu Sightseeing Bus (via Manza-Kashizawa-guchi Station) from {{ill|Karuizawa Station|ja|軽井沢駅|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Hokuriku Shinkansen|ja|北陸新幹線|12=draft}}. * There are three bus stops in the Manza Onsen area: "Manza Onsen" (near the entrance), the central "Manza Prince Hotel" bus stop, and the "Manza Onsen Ue" bus stop. * Automobile: : The only road available year‑round is the paid {{ill|Manza Highway|ja|万座ハイウェー|12=draft}}, about 22 km from Mihara in Tsumagoi Village. : From spring to autumn, {{ill|National Route 292|ja|国道292号|12=draft}} (Shiga‑Kusatsu Kogen Route) is available (it is closed in winter). Note that due to the eruption alert level for {{ill|Kusatsu Shirane|ja|草津白根山|12=draft}}, the section between the (Kusatsu) {{ill|Sesshōgahara|ja|殺生河原|12=draft}} parking area and the Manza Sansaji intersection may be closed. As of April 2019, the eruption alert level is 2; although normally it would be closed all day, an exception allows passage during daytime only (bicycles, motorcycles, and convertibles are not permitted; weather conditions such as fog may also cause closures).

According to the reports of the {{ill|Naganohara Police Station|ja|長野原警察署|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Agatsuma Western Regional Fire Department|ja|吾妻広域西部消防署|12=draft}}, although 135 guests were staying in the old building, the deep snow enabled most to escape by jumping from the windows, preventing a far worse tragedy<ref name="mainichi">[[ill|Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞]] Evening Edition, February 23, 1983</ref>.
In 2004, during the hot spring misrepresentation scandal, a ryokan was reported to have displayed descriptions on its website suggesting hot spring usage despite not drawing any; subsequent corrections were made. As of 2019, the management of the affected facility has changed.
== Access == * Railway ({{ill|JR East|ja|JR東日本|12=draft}}) : Approximately 40 minutes by {{ill|Seibu Sightseeing Bus|ja|西武観光バス|12=draft}} from {{ill|Manza-Kashizawa-guchi Station|ja|万座・鹿沢口駅|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Agatsuma Line|ja|吾妻線|12=draft}}. : Approximately 90 minutes by Seibu Sightseeing Bus (via Manza-Kashizawa-guchi Station) from {{ill|Karuizawa Station|ja|軽井沢駅|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Hokuriku Shinkansen|ja|北陸新幹線|12=draft}}. * There are three bus stops in the Manza Onsen area: "Manza Onsen" (near the entrance), the central "Manza Prince Hotel" bus stop, and the "Manza Onsen Ue" bus stop. * Automobile: : The only road available year‑round is the paid {{ill|Manza Highway|ja|万座ハイウェー|12=draft}}, about 22 km from Mihara in Tsumagoi Village. : From spring to autumn, {{ill|National Route 292|ja|国道292号|12=draft}} (Shiga‑Kusatsu Kogen Route) is available (it is closed in winter). Note that due to the eruption alert level for {{ill|Kusatsu Shirane|ja|草津白根山|12=draft}}, the section between the (Kusatsu) {{ill|Sesshōgahara|ja|殺生河原|12=draft}} parking area and the Manza Sansaji intersection may be closed. As of April 2019, the eruption alert level is 2; although normally it would be closed all day, an exception allows passage during daytime only (bicycles, motorcycles, and convertibles are not permitted; weather conditions such as fog may also cause closures).
== Footnotes ==
<references />
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Manza Onsen Ski Resort|ja|万座温泉スキー場|12=draft}} * {{ill|Manza Prince Hotel|ja|万座プリンスホテル|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉|12=draft}} * {{ill|ii Yudana|ja|いい湯だな|12=draft}} ({{ill|Duke Aces|ja|デューク・エイセス|12=draft}}) – Third set of lyrics
== External Links == * [http://www.manzaonsen.gr.jp/ Manza Onsen Tourism Association]

{{出典の明記|date=March 2017}}{{infobox Onsen | 名称 = Manza Onsen | native_name = 万座温泉 | image = Manza Onsen Tsumagoi Gunma01bs4272.jpg | コメント = Manza-yama and Manza Onsen | 所在地 = {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|群馬県|12=draft}} {{ill|Agatsuma District|ja|吾妻郡|12=draft}} {{ill|Tsumagoi Village|ja|嬬恋村|12=draft}} 干俣 | 緯度度 = 36 | 緯度分 = 38 | 緯度秒 = 5.1 | 経度度 = 138 | 経度分 = 30 | 経度秒 = 31.7 | 交通アクセス = ''See the [[#Access|Access]] section.'' | 泉質 = Acidic sulfur spring | 泉温 = Approximately 80°C | 湧出量 = Approximately 5,400,000 {{ill|L|ja|リットル|12=draft}}/day | pH = | 液性の分類 = | 浸透圧の分類 = | 宿泊施設数 = 9 | 総収容人員数 = | 年間浴客数 = | 統計年度 = | 外部リンク = http://www.manzaonsen.gr.jp/ | 特記事項 = }}
[[File:Kusatsu-Shiranesan01s5s4272.jpg|thumb|image]] '''Manza Onsen''' (万座温泉) is a hot spring located in Hoshimata, {{ill|Tsumagoi Village|ja|嬬恋村|12=draft}}, {{ill|Agatsuma District|ja|吾妻郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|群馬県|12=draft}}, within the {{ill|Joshinetsu Kogen National Park|ja|上信越高原国立公園|12=draft}}, at an elevation of 1,800 m. It is one of the highest hot springs in Japan that can be reached year‑round by private automobile, along with {{ill|Daku River Onsen|ja|濁河温泉|12=draft}} in {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県|12=draft}}’s {{ill|Gero City|ja|下呂市|12=draft}}. Nearby are three explosive crater lakes: Yubatake, Karagama, and Mizukama.
== Water Quality == * Acidic sulfur spring ({{ill|Hydrogen Sulfide Spring|ja|硫化水素泉|12=draft}}) : The water is characterized by its milky white or yellowish appearance and contains the highest sulfur content of any hot spring in Japan. It has a discharge of 3,750 L per minute.
== Onsen Town == [[File:Manza ski resort Mt.Asahi 20080219.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Manza Onsen Ski Resort|ja|万座温泉スキー場|12=draft}}]] Located on the slopes of {{ill|Kusatsu Shirane|ja|草津白根山|12=draft}}, Manza Onsen was once a thriving hot spring resort ({{ill|Yūji|ja|湯治場|12=draft}}) from spring through autumn and is now renowned as a ski destination in winter. The high temperature of its sources causes a continuous plume of steam, and the source areas display a hellish appearance with a pervasive sulfur odor. Hot spring sources emerge abundantly along the riverbed<ref name="noguchi">[[ill|Fuyuhito Noguchi|ja|野口冬人]]『湯治の宿』, Yomiuri Travel Publishing</ref>. A notable vent known as "Karabuki" near the outskirts of the central area is a local specialty (though its steam output has diminished since around 2012). The gently sloping mountainside is dotted with inns and hotels, with the {{ill|Manza Onsen Ski Resort|ja|万座温泉スキー場|12=draft}} situated at the rear. Because the area is within a national park, facility expansions or renovations are limited, resulting in many aging structures. Some accommodations have closed or been absorbed by others; as of 2019, only 7 inns and hotels continue independent operations for individual guests. There are also lodging options with self-catering facilities for long-term hot spring therapy, though no convenience stores exist in the area apart from small shops attached to the accommodations. Additionally, the Manza Onsen Ski Resort was featured as a filming location for the movie [[Take Me Skiing]]. There are over 10 hot spring sources, including Yubatake, Banna no Yu, Daiku Yu, Tetsuyu, and Hōsei no Yu.
== History == The period of initial development is unclear; however, Yayoi pottery has been excavated in the vicinity. Legend has it that {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂|12=draft}} exorcised demons here, and records indicate that samurai visited for hot spring therapy during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=draft}}. Although it operated as a hot spring resort during the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, it did not flourish until after World War II, when the {{ill|Seibu Group|ja|西武グループ|12=draft}} undertook resort development and transportation improved.
On February 22, 1983, the old building of Manza Onsen Hotel (Nisshinkan) caught fire, completely burning an area of 1,000 m². Four ski guests sustained minor injuries. According to the reports of the {{ill|Naganohara Police Station|ja|長野原警察署|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Agatsuma Western Regional Fire Department|ja|吾妻広域西部消防署|12=draft}}, although 135 guests were staying in the old building, the deep snow enabled most to escape by jumping from the windows, preventing a far worse tragedy<ref name="mainichi">[[ill|Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞]] Evening Edition, February 23, 1983</ref>.
In 2004, during the hot spring misrepresentation scandal, a ryokan was reported to have displayed descriptions on its website suggesting hot spring usage despite not drawing any; subsequent corrections were made. As of 2019, the management of the affected facility has changed.
== Access == * Railway ({{ill|JR East|ja|JR東日本|12=draft}}) : Approximately 40 minutes by {{ill|Seibu Sightseeing Bus|ja|西武観光バス|12=draft}} from {{ill|Manza-Kashizawa-guchi Station|ja|万座・鹿沢口駅|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Agatsuma Line|ja|吾妻線|12=draft}}. : Approximately 90 minutes by Seibu Sightseeing Bus (via Manza-Kashizawa-guchi Station) from {{ill|Karuizawa Station|ja|軽井沢駅|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Hokuriku Shinkansen|ja|北陸新幹線|12=draft}}. * There are three bus stops in the Manza Onsen area: "Manza Onsen" (near the entrance), the central "Manza Prince Hotel" bus stop, and the "Manza Onsen Ue" bus stop. * Automobile: : The only road available year‑round is the paid {{ill|Manza Highway|ja|万座ハイウェー|12=draft}}, about 22 km from Mihara in Tsumagoi Village. : From spring to autumn, {{ill|National Route 292|ja|国道292号|12=draft}} (Shiga‑Kusatsu Kogen Route) is available (it is closed in winter). Note that due to the eruption alert level for {{ill|Kusatsu Shirane|ja|草津白根山|12=draft}}, the section between the (Kusatsu) {{ill|Sesshōgahara|ja|殺生河原|12=draft}} parking area and the Manza Sansaji intersection may be closed. As of April 2019, the eruption alert level is 2; although normally it would be closed all day, an exception allows passage during daytime only (bicycles, motorcycles, and convertibles are not permitted; weather conditions such as fog may also cause closures).
== Footnotes ==
<references />
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Manza Onsen Ski Resort|ja|万座温泉スキー場|12=draft}} * {{ill|Manza Prince Hotel|ja|万座プリンスホテル|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉|12=draft}} * {{ill|ii Yudana|ja|いい湯だな|12=draft}} ({{ill|Duke Aces|ja|デューク・エイセス|12=draft}}) – Third set of lyrics

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2017年3月}}{{日本の温泉地 |名称 = 万座温泉 |画像 = Manza Onsen Tsumagoi Gunma01bs4272.jpg |コメント = 万座山と万座温泉 |所在地 = [[群馬県]][[吾妻郡]][[嬬恋村]]干俣 | 緯度度 = 36 | 緯度分 = 38 | 緯度秒 = 5.1 | 経度度 = 138 |経度分 = 30 | 経度秒 = 31.7 |交通アクセス = ''[[#アクセス|アクセス]]の項を参照のこと'' |泉質 = 酸性硫黄泉 |泉温 = 約80 |湧出量 = 約5,400,000 [[リットル|L]]/日 |pH = |液性の分類 = |浸透圧の分類 = |宿泊施設数 = 9 |総収容人員数 = |年間浴客数 = |統計年度 = |外部リンク = [http://www.manzaonsen.gr.jp/ 万座温泉観光協会] |特記事項 = }} [[ファイル:Kusatsu-Shiranesan01s5s4272.jpg|thumb|湯釜]] '''万座温泉'''（まんざおんせん）は、[[群馬県]][[吾妻郡]][[嬬恋村]]干俣（ほしまた）（旧国[[上野国]]）にある[[上信越高原国立公園]]内、標高1,800mに位置する[[温泉]]。[[岐阜県]][[下呂市]]の[[濁河温泉]]と並び通年自家用車で辿り着ける日本最高所の温泉である。近くには[[草津白根山|湯釜]]・涸釜・水釜の3つの[[爆裂火口]]湖がある。
== 泉質 == * 酸性硫黄泉（[[硫黄泉|硫化水素泉]]） : 白濁、または黄濁した湯が特徴であり、[[硫黄]]成分の含有量が日本一の温泉である。毎分3,750Lの湧出量がある。
== 温泉街 == [[ファイル:Manza ski resort Mt.Asahi 20080219.jpg|thumb|[[万座温泉スキー場]]]] [[草津白根山]]山麓に位置し、冬は[[スキー]]のメッカとして[[春]]から[[秋]]にかけてはかつては[[湯治場]]としてにぎわった。[[源泉]]温度が高いことからもうもうと湯けむりを上げ、源泉地は地獄的な様相を見せ、[[硫黄]]の匂いが充満している。川床には至る所に源泉が湧出している<ref name="noguchi">[[野口冬人]]『湯治の宿』読売旅行出版社</ref>。中心部の外れにある「空吹」(からぶき)と呼ばれる水蒸気を含んだ[[硫化水素]]煙の噴出口は万座温泉の名物となっている(2012年頃から噴出量が減少し以前ほどの迫力はなくなっている)。なだらかな山の斜面に旅館・ホテルが点在し、後背には[[万座温泉スキー場]]がある。一帯が[[国立公園]]のため、施設増改築などが思うようにできず、やや老朽化した施設が多い。廃業・他社に吸収された宿泊施設もあり、2019年現在は個人客向け営業を行っている旅館・ホテルは7軒である。長期滞在の[[湯治]]客のために、自炊のできる宿泊施設もあるが、地区内は[[コンビニエンスストア]]がなく各宿泊施設の売店しかない。また、万座温泉スキー場は映画「[[私をスキーに連れてって]]」の撮影地にもなった。湯畑・姥の湯・大苦湯・鉄湯・法性の湯など10ケ所あまりの源泉がある。
== 歴史 == 開湯時期は不明である。周辺では[[弥生土器]]が出土している。[[坂上田村麻呂]]が鬼退治をした伝説があり、[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]には武士が湯治に訪れた記録もある。[[江戸時代]]にも湯治場として開けていたが、あまり栄えてはいない。万座温泉が本格的に開けてくるのは、戦後になって[[西武グループ]]による[[リゾート]]開発が行われ、交通の便が改善されてからである。
[[1983年]][[2月22日]]、万座温泉ホテル(日進館)旧館から出火し、1000m{{Sup|2}}を全焼。スキー客4人が軽傷を負った。[[硫黄]]で錆びた[[火災報知器]]が作動せず、適マークもなかった。[[長野原警察署]]と[[吾妻広域町村圏振興整備組合|吾妻広域西部消防署]]によると、旧館には満員の135名が宿泊して寝込んでいたが、雪が深かったことが幸いしてほとんどの客が窓から飛び降りたため惨事にはならなかった<ref name="mainichi">[[毎日新聞]][[夕刊]]1983年2月23日</ref>。
[[2004年]]に発生した[[温泉偽装問題]]では、温泉を引いていないにもかかわらず、ウェブサイト上に温泉利用を思わせる表記を行っていた旅館があったと報じられた。以後、当該施設での表記は改善された。2019年現在では当該施設はいずれも経営が変わっている。
== アクセス == * 鉄道（[[東日本旅客鉄道|JR東日本]]） : [[吾妻線]][[万座・鹿沢口駅]]より[[西武観光バス]]で約40分。 : [[北陸新幹線]][[軽井沢駅]]より西武観光バスで約90分。（万座・鹿沢口駅経由） :* 万座温泉地区には手前から「万座温泉」[[バス停留所]]、中心部となる「[[万座プリンスホテル]]」バス停留所、その後に「万座温泉上」バス停留所と3箇所のバス停留所がある。 * 自動車 : 通年利用できるのは有料道路の[[万座ハイウェー]]のみ（嬬恋村三原から約22kmの距離）。 : 春季～秋季は[[国道292号|志賀草津高原ルート]]が利用できる(冬季は通行止め)。なお[[草津白根山]]の噴火警戒レベルの影響により、(草津)[[殺生河原]]駐車場～万座三差路間は通行止めとなることがある。2019年4月現在は噴火警戒レベル2であり本来なら終日通行止めとするところ、例外的に日中のみ通行可としている(自転車・バイク・オープンカー等は通行不可)(霧等の気象条件によって通行止めとなることがある)。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[万座温泉スキー場]] * [[万座プリンスホテル]] * [[草津温泉]] * [[いい湯だな]]（[[デューク・エイセス]]）- 3番目の歌詞
== 外部リンク == * [http://www.manzaonsen.gr.jp/ 万座温泉観光協会]
{{温泉}} {{Normdaten}}
{{デフォルトソート:まんさおんせん}} [[Category:群馬県の温泉]] [[Category:嬬恋村の地理]]

== 温泉街 == [[ファイル:Manza ski resort Mt.Asahi 20080219.jpg|thumb|[[万座温泉スキー場]]]] [[草津白根山]]山麓に位置し、冬は[[スキー]]のメッカとして[[春]]から[[秋]]にかけてはかつては[[湯治場]]としてにぎわった。[[源泉]]温度が高いことからもうもうと湯けむりを上げ、源泉地は地獄的な様相を見せ、[[硫黄]]の匂いが充満している。川床には至る所に源泉が湧出している<ref name="noguchi">[[野口冬人]]『湯治の宿』読売旅行出版社</ref>。中心部の外れにある「空吹」(からぶき)と呼ばれる水蒸気を含んだ[[硫化水素]]煙の噴出口は万座温泉の名物となっている(2012年頃から噴出量が減少し以前ほどの迫力はなくなっている)。なだらかな山の斜面に旅館・ホテルが点在し、後背には[[万座温泉スキー場]]がある。一帯が[[国立公園]]のため、施設増改築などが思うようにできず、やや老朽化した施設が多い。廃業・他社に吸収された宿泊施設もあり、2019年現在は個人客向け営業を行っている旅館・ホテルは7軒である。長期滞在の[[湯治]]客のために、自炊のできる宿泊施設もあるが、地区内は[[コンビニエンスストア]]がなく各宿泊施設の売店しかない。また、万座温泉スキー場は映画「[[私をスキーに連れてって]]」の撮影地にもなった。湯畑・姥の湯・大苦湯・鉄湯・法性の湯など10ケ所あまりの源泉がある。
== 歴史 == 開湯時期は不明である。周辺では[[弥生土器]]が出土している。[[坂上田村麻呂]]が鬼退治をした伝説があり、[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]には武士が湯治に訪れた記録もある。[[江戸時代]]にも湯治場として開けていたが、あまり栄えてはいない。万座温泉が本格的に開けてくるのは、戦後になって[[西武グループ]]による[[リゾート]]開発が行われ、交通の便が改善されてからである。
[[1983年]][[2月22日]]、万座温泉ホテル(日進館)旧館から出火し、1000m{{Sup|2}}を全焼。スキー客4人が軽傷を負った。[[硫黄]]で錆びた[[火災報知器]]が作動せず、適マークもなかった。[[長野原警察署]]と[[吾妻広域町村圏振興整備組合|吾妻広域西部消防署]]によると、旧館には満員の135名が宿泊して寝込んでいたが、雪が深かったことが幸いしてほとんどの客が窓から飛び降りたため惨事にはならなかった<ref name="mainichi">[[毎日新聞]][[夕刊]]1983年2月23日</ref>。
[[2004年]]に発生した[[温泉偽装問題]]では、温泉を引いていないにもかかわらず、ウェブサイト上に温泉利用を思わせる表記を行っていた旅館があったと報じられた。以後、当該施設での表記は改善された。2019年現在では当該施設はいずれも経営が変わっている。
== アクセス == * 鉄道（[[東日本旅客鉄道|JR東日本]]） : [[吾妻線]][[万座・鹿沢口駅]]より[[西武観光バス]]で約40分。 : [[北陸新幹線]][[軽井沢駅]]より西武観光バスで約90分。（万座・鹿沢口駅経由） :* 万座温泉地区には手前から「万座温泉」[[バス停留所]]、中心部となる「[[万座プリンスホテル]]」バス停留所、その後に「万座温泉上」バス停留所と3箇所のバス停留所がある。 * 自動車 : 通年利用できるのは有料道路の[[万座ハイウェー]]のみ（嬬恋村三原から約22kmの距離）。 : 春季～秋季は[[国道292号|志賀草津高原ルート]]が利用できる(冬季は通行止め)。なお[[草津白根山]]の噴火警戒レベルの影響により、(草津)[[殺生河原]]駐車場～万座三差路間は通行止めとなることがある。2019年4月現在は噴火警戒レベル2であり本来なら終日通行止めとするところ、例外的に日中のみ通行可としている(自転車・バイク・オープンカー等は通行不可)(霧等の気象条件によって通行止めとなることがある)。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[万座温泉スキー場]] * [[万座プリンスホテル]] * [[草津温泉]] * [[いい湯だな]]（[[デューク・エイセス]]）- 3番目の歌詞
== 外部リンク == * [http://www.manzaonsen.gr.jp/ 万座温泉観光協会]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Manza Onsen]][[ja:万座温泉]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11353839]] Wikidata name: Manza Onsen [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Referencesの明記|date=March 2017}}{{infobox Onsen | 名称 = Manza Onsen | native_name = 万座温泉 | image = Manza Onsen Tsumagoi Gunma01bs4272.jpg | コメント = Manza-yama and Manza Onsen | 所在地 = [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]] [[:en:Agatsuma District, Gunma|Agatsuma District]] [[:en:Tsumagoi|Tsumagoi Village]] 干俣 | 緯度度 = 36 | 緯度分 = 38 | 緯度秒 = 5.1 | 経度度 = 138 | 経度分 = 30 | 経度秒 = 31.7 | 交通アクセス = ''See the [[#Access|Access]] section.'' | 泉質 = Acidic sulfur spring | 泉温 = Approximately 80°C | 湧出量 = Approximately 5,400,000 [[:en:Litre|L]]/day | pH = | 液性の分類 = | 浸透圧の分類 = | 宿泊施設数 = 9 | 総収容人員数 = | 年間浴客数 = | 統計年度 = | External links = http://www.manzaonsen.gr.jp/ | 特記事項 = }}
[[File:Kusatsu-Shiranesan01s5s4272.jpg|thumb|image]] '''Manza Onsen''' (万座温泉) is a hot spring located in Hoshimata, [[:en:Tsumagoi|Tsumagoi Village]], [[:en:Agatsuma District, Gunma|Agatsuma District]], [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]], within the [[:en:Jōshin'etsu-kōgen National Park|Joshinetsu Kogen National Park]], at an elevation of 1,800 m. It is one of the highest hot springs in Japan that can be reached year‑round by private automobile, along with {{ill|Daku River Onsen|ja|濁河温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daku River Onsen|qq=|1=Nigorigo Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566108}} in [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]]’s [[:en:Gero, Gifu|Gero City]]. Nearby are three explosive crater lakes: Yubatake, Karagama, and Mizukama.
== Water Quality == * Acidic sulfur spring ({{ill|Hydrogen Sulfide Spring|ja|硫化水素泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hydrogen Sulfide Spring|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 硫黄泉|1=Sulfur Spring|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11587679}}) : The water is characterized by its milky white or yellowish appearance and contains the highest sulfur content of any hot spring in Japan. It has a discharge of 3,750 L per minute.
== Onsen Town == [[File:Manza ski resort Mt.Asahi 20080219.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Manza Onsen Ski Resort|ja|万座温泉スキー場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Onsen Ski Resort|qq=|1=Manza Onsen Ski Resort|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353840}}]] Located on the slopes of [[:en:Mount Kusatsu-Shirane|Kusatsu Shirane]], Manza Onsen was once a thriving hot spring resort ({{ill|Yūji|ja|湯治場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yūji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 湯治|1=thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}}) from spring through autumn and is now renowned as a ski destination in winter. The high temperature of its sources causes a continuous plume of steam, and the source areas display a hellish appearance with a pervasive sulfur odor. Hot spring sources emerge abundantly along the riverbed<ref name="noguchi">[[:en:Ill|Fuyuhito Noguchi|ja|野口冬人]]『湯治の宿』, Yomiuri Travel Publishing</ref>. A notable vent known as "Karabuki" near the outskirts of the central area is a local specialty (though its steam output has diminished since around 2012). The gently sloping mountainside is dotted with inns and hotels, with the {{ill|Manza Onsen Ski Resort|ja|万座温泉スキー場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Onsen Ski Resort|qq=|1=Manza Onsen Ski Resort|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353840}} situated at the rear. Because the area is within a national park, facility expansions or renovations are limited, resulting in many aging structures. Some accommodations have closed or been absorbed by others; as of 2019, only 7 inns and hotels continue independent operations for individual guests. There are also lodging options with self-catering facilities for long-term hot spring therapy, though no convenience stores exist in the area apart from small shops attached to the accommodations. Additionally, the Manza Onsen Ski Resort was featured as a filming location for the movie [[Take Me Skiing]]. There are over 10 hot spring sources, including Yubatake, Banna no Yu, Daiku Yu, Tetsuyu, and Hōsei no Yu.
== History == The period of initial development is unclear; however, Yayoi pottery has been excavated in the vicinity. Legend has it that [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] exorcised demons here, and records indicate that samurai visited for hot spring therapy during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}. Although it operated as a hot spring resort during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], it did not flourish until after World War II, when the {{ill|Seibu Group|ja|西武グループ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seibu Group|qq=|1=Seibu Group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628875}} undertook resort development and transportation improved.
On February 22, 1983, the old building of Manza Onsen Hotel (Nisshinkan) caught fire, completely burning an area of 1,000 m². Four ski guests sustained minor injuries. According to the reports of the {{ill|Naganohara Police Station|ja|長野原警察署|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naganohara Police Station|qq=|1=Naganohara Police Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11654145}} and the {{ill|Agatsuma Western Regional Fire Department|ja|吾妻広域西部消防署|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Agatsuma Western Regional Fire Department|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, although 135 guests were staying in the old building, the deep snow enabled most to escape by jumping from the windows, preventing a far worse tragedy<ref name="mainichi">[[:en:Ill|Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞]] Evening Edition, February 23, 1983</ref>.
In 2004, during the hot spring misrepresentation scandal, a ryokan was reported to have displayed descriptions on its website suggesting hot spring usage despite not drawing any; subsequent corrections were made. As of 2019, the management of the affected facility has changed.
== Access == * Railway ({{ill|JR East|ja|JR東日本|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR East|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東日本旅客鉄道|1=East Japan Railway Company|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q499071}}) : Approximately 40 minutes by {{ill|Seibu Sightseeing Bus|ja|西武観光バス|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seibu Sightseeing Bus|qq=|1=Seibu Kanko Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628934}} from [[:en:Manza-Kazawaguchi Station|Manza-Kashizawa-guchi Station]] on the [[:en:Agatsuma Line|Agatsuma Line]]. : Approximately 90 minutes by Seibu Sightseeing Bus (via Manza-Kashizawa-guchi Station) from [[:en:Karuizawa Station|Karuizawa Station]] on the [[:en:Hokuriku Shinkansen|Hokuriku Shinkansen]]. * There are three bus stops in the Manza Onsen area: "Manza Onsen" (near the entrance), the central "Manza Prince Hotel" bus stop, and the "Manza Onsen Ue" bus stop. * Automobile: : The only road available year‑round is the paid {{ill|Manza Highway|ja|万座ハイウェー|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Highway|qq=|1=Manza highway|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353835}}, about 22 km from Mihara in Tsumagoi Village. : From spring to autumn, [[:en:Japan National Route 292|National Route 292]] (Shiga‑Kusatsu Kogen Route) is available (it is closed in winter). Note that due to the eruption alert level for [[:en:Mount Kusatsu-Shirane|Kusatsu Shirane]], the section between the (Kusatsu) {{ill|Sesshōgahara|ja|殺生河原|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sesshōgahara|qq=|1=Sesshōgawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q105410918}} parking area and the Manza Sansaji intersection may be closed. As of April 2019, the eruption alert level is 2; although normally it would be closed all day, an exception allows passage during daytime only (bicycles, motorcycles, and convertibles are not permitted; weather conditions such as fog may also cause closures).
== Footnotes ==
<references />
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Manza Onsen Ski Resort|ja|万座温泉スキー場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Onsen Ski Resort|qq=|1=Manza Onsen Ski Resort|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353840}} * {{ill|Manza Prince Hotel|ja|万座プリンスホテル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Prince Hotel|qq=|1=Manza Prince Hotel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353836}} * [[:en:Kusatsu Onsen|Kusatsu Onsen]] * {{ill|ii Yudana|ja|いい湯だな|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ii Yudana|qq=|1=ii Yudana|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11259008}} ({{ill|Duke Aces|ja|デューク・エイセス|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Duke Aces|qq=|1=Duke Aces|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11320788}}) – Third set of lyrics
== External Links == * [http://www.manzaonsen.gr.jp/ Manza Onsen Tourism Association]

According to the reports of the {{ill|Naganohara Police Station|ja|長野原警察署|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naganohara Police Station|qq=|1=Naganohara Police Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11654145}} and the {{ill|Agatsuma Western Regional Fire Department|ja|吾妻広域西部消防署|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Agatsuma Western Regional Fire Department|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, although 135 guests were staying in the old building, the deep snow enabled most to escape by jumping from the windows, preventing a far worse tragedy<ref name="mainichi">[[:en:Ill|Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞]] Evening Edition, February 23, 1983</ref>.
In 2004, during the hot spring misrepresentation scandal, a ryokan was reported to have displayed descriptions on its website suggesting hot spring usage despite not drawing any; subsequent corrections were made. As of 2019, the management of the affected facility has changed.
== Access == * Railway ({{ill|JR East|ja|JR東日本|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR East|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東日本旅客鉄道|1=East Japan Railway Company|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q499071}}) : Approximately 40 minutes by {{ill|Seibu Sightseeing Bus|ja|西武観光バス|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seibu Sightseeing Bus|qq=|1=Seibu Kanko Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628934}} from [[:en:Manza-Kazawaguchi Station|Manza-Kashizawa-guchi Station]] on the [[:en:Agatsuma Line|Agatsuma Line]]. : Approximately 90 minutes by Seibu Sightseeing Bus (via Manza-Kashizawa-guchi Station) from [[:en:Karuizawa Station|Karuizawa Station]] on the [[:en:Hokuriku Shinkansen|Hokuriku Shinkansen]]. * There are three bus stops in the Manza Onsen area: "Manza Onsen" (near the entrance), the central "Manza Prince Hotel" bus stop, and the "Manza Onsen Ue" bus stop. * Automobile: : The only road available year‑round is the paid {{ill|Manza Highway|ja|万座ハイウェー|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Highway|qq=|1=Manza highway|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353835}}, about 22 km from Mihara in Tsumagoi Village. : From spring to autumn, [[:en:Japan National Route 292|National Route 292]] (Shiga‑Kusatsu Kogen Route) is available (it is closed in winter). Note that due to the eruption alert level for [[:en:Mount Kusatsu-Shirane|Kusatsu Shirane]], the section between the (Kusatsu) {{ill|Sesshōgahara|ja|殺生河原|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sesshōgahara|qq=|1=Sesshōgawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q105410918}} parking area and the Manza Sansaji intersection may be closed. As of April 2019, the eruption alert level is 2; although normally it would be closed all day, an exception allows passage during daytime only (bicycles, motorcycles, and convertibles are not permitted; weather conditions such as fog may also cause closures).

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Manza Onsen]][[ja:万座温泉]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11353839]] Wikidata name: Manza Onsen [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Referencesの明記|date=March 2017}}{{infobox Onsen | 名称 = Manza Onsen | native_name = 万座温泉 | image = Manza Onsen Tsumagoi Gunma01bs4272.jpg | コメント = Manza-yama and Manza Onsen | 所在地 = [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]] [[:en:Agatsuma District, Gunma|Agatsuma District]] [[:en:Tsumagoi|Tsumagoi Village]] 干俣 | 緯度度 = 36 | 緯度分 = 38 | 緯度秒 = 5.1 | 経度度 = 138 | 経度分 = 30 | 経度秒 = 31.7 | 交通アクセス = ''See the [[#Access|Access]] section.'' | 泉質 = Acidic sulfur spring | 泉温 = Approximately 80°C | 湧出量 = Approximately 5,400,000 [[:en:Litre|L]]/day | pH = | 液性の分類 = | 浸透圧の分類 = | 宿泊施設数 = 9 | 総収容人員数 = | 年間浴客数 = | 統計年度 = | External links = http://www.manzaonsen.gr.jp/ | 特記事項 = }}
[[File:Kusatsu-Shiranesan01s5s4272.jpg|thumb|image]] '''Manza Onsen''' (万座温泉) is a hot spring located in Hoshimata, [[:en:Tsumagoi|Tsumagoi Village]], [[:en:Agatsuma District, Gunma|Agatsuma District]], [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]], within the [[:en:Jōshin'etsu-kōgen National Park|Joshinetsu Kogen National Park]], at an elevation of 1,800 m. It is one of the highest hot springs in Japan that can be reached year‑round by private automobile, along with {{ill|Daku River Onsen|ja|濁河温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daku River Onsen|qq=|1=Nigorigo Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566108}} in [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]]’s [[:en:Gero, Gifu|Gero City]]. Nearby are three explosive crater lakes: Yubatake, Karagama, and Mizukama.
== Water Quality == * Acidic sulfur spring ({{ill|Hydrogen Sulfide Spring|ja|硫化水素泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hydrogen Sulfide Spring|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 硫黄泉|1=Sulfur Spring|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11587679}}) : The water is characterized by its milky white or yellowish appearance and contains the highest sulfur content of any hot spring in Japan. It has a discharge of 3,750 L per minute.
== Onsen Town == [[File:Manza ski resort Mt.Asahi 20080219.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Manza Onsen Ski Resort|ja|万座温泉スキー場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Onsen Ski Resort|qq=|1=Manza Onsen Ski Resort|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353840}}]] Located on the slopes of [[:en:Mount Kusatsu-Shirane|Kusatsu Shirane]], Manza Onsen was once a thriving hot spring resort ({{ill|Yūji|ja|湯治場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yūji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 湯治|1=thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}}) from spring through autumn and is now renowned as a ski destination in winter. The high temperature of its sources causes a continuous plume of steam, and the source areas display a hellish appearance with a pervasive sulfur odor. Hot spring sources emerge abundantly along the riverbed<ref name="noguchi">[[:en:Ill|Fuyuhito Noguchi|ja|野口冬人]]『湯治の宿』, Yomiuri Travel Publishing</ref>. A notable vent known as "Karabuki" near the outskirts of the central area is a local specialty (though its steam output has diminished since around 2012). The gently sloping mountainside is dotted with inns and hotels, with the {{ill|Manza Onsen Ski Resort|ja|万座温泉スキー場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Onsen Ski Resort|qq=|1=Manza Onsen Ski Resort|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353840}} situated at the rear. Because the area is within a national park, facility expansions or renovations are limited, resulting in many aging structures. Some accommodations have closed or been absorbed by others; as of 2019, only 7 inns and hotels continue independent operations for individual guests. There are also lodging options with self-catering facilities for long-term hot spring therapy, though no convenience stores exist in the area apart from small shops attached to the accommodations. Additionally, the Manza Onsen Ski Resort was featured as a filming location for the movie [[Take Me Skiing]]. There are over 10 hot spring sources, including Yubatake, Banna no Yu, Daiku Yu, Tetsuyu, and Hōsei no Yu.
== History == The period of initial development is unclear; however, Yayoi pottery has been excavated in the vicinity. Legend has it that [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] exorcised demons here, and records indicate that samurai visited for hot spring therapy during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}. Although it operated as a hot spring resort during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], it did not flourish until after World War II, when the {{ill|Seibu Group|ja|西武グループ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seibu Group|qq=|1=Seibu Group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628875}} undertook resort development and transportation improved.
On February 22, 1983, the old building of Manza Onsen Hotel (Nisshinkan) caught fire, completely burning an area of 1,000 m². Four ski guests sustained minor injuries. According to the reports of the {{ill|Naganohara Police Station|ja|長野原警察署|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naganohara Police Station|qq=|1=Naganohara Police Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11654145}} and the {{ill|Agatsuma Western Regional Fire Department|ja|吾妻広域西部消防署|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Agatsuma Western Regional Fire Department|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, although 135 guests were staying in the old building, the deep snow enabled most to escape by jumping from the windows, preventing a far worse tragedy<ref name="mainichi">[[:en:Ill|Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞]] Evening Edition, February 23, 1983</ref>.
In 2004, during the hot spring misrepresentation scandal, a ryokan was reported to have displayed descriptions on its website suggesting hot spring usage despite not drawing any; subsequent corrections were made. As of 2019, the management of the affected facility has changed.
== Access == * Railway ({{ill|JR East|ja|JR東日本|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR East|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東日本旅客鉄道|1=East Japan Railway Company|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q499071}}) : Approximately 40 minutes by {{ill|Seibu Sightseeing Bus|ja|西武観光バス|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seibu Sightseeing Bus|qq=|1=Seibu Kanko Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628934}} from [[:en:Manza-Kazawaguchi Station|Manza-Kashizawa-guchi Station]] on the [[:en:Agatsuma Line|Agatsuma Line]]. : Approximately 90 minutes by Seibu Sightseeing Bus (via Manza-Kashizawa-guchi Station) from [[:en:Karuizawa Station|Karuizawa Station]] on the [[:en:Hokuriku Shinkansen|Hokuriku Shinkansen]]. * There are three bus stops in the Manza Onsen area: "Manza Onsen" (near the entrance), the central "Manza Prince Hotel" bus stop, and the "Manza Onsen Ue" bus stop. * Automobile: : The only road available year‑round is the paid {{ill|Manza Highway|ja|万座ハイウェー|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Highway|qq=|1=Manza highway|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353835}}, about 22 km from Mihara in Tsumagoi Village. : From spring to autumn, [[:en:Japan National Route 292|National Route 292]] (Shiga‑Kusatsu Kogen Route) is available (it is closed in winter). Note that due to the eruption alert level for [[:en:Mount Kusatsu-Shirane|Kusatsu Shirane]], the section between the (Kusatsu) {{ill|Sesshōgahara|ja|殺生河原|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sesshōgahara|qq=|1=Sesshōgawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q105410918}} parking area and the Manza Sansaji intersection may be closed. As of April 2019, the eruption alert level is 2; although normally it would be closed all day, an exception allows passage during daytime only (bicycles, motorcycles, and convertibles are not permitted; weather conditions such as fog may also cause closures).

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Manza Onsen]][[ja:万座温泉]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11353839]] Wikidata name: Manza Onsen [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Referencesの明記|date=March 2017}}{{infobox Onsen | 名称 = Manza Onsen | native_name = 万座温泉 | image = Manza Onsen Tsumagoi Gunma01bs4272.jpg | コメント = Manza-yama and Manza Onsen | 所在地 = [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]] [[:en:Agatsuma District, Gunma|Agatsuma District]] [[:en:Tsumagoi|Tsumagoi Village]] 干俣 | 緯度度 = 36 | 緯度分 = 38 | 緯度秒 = 5.1 | 経度度 = 138 | 経度分 = 30 | 経度秒 = 31.7 | 交通アクセス = ''See the [[#Access|Access]] section.'' | 泉質 = Acidic sulfur spring | 泉温 = Approximately 80°C | 湧出量 = Approximately 5,400,000 [[:en:Litre|L]]/day | pH = | 液性の分類 = | 浸透圧の分類 = | 宿泊施設数 = 9 | 総収容人員数 = | 年間浴客数 = | 統計年度 = | External links = http://www.manzaonsen.gr.jp/ | 特記事項 = }}
[[File:Kusatsu-Shiranesan01s5s4272.jpg|thumb|image]] '''Manza Onsen''' (万座温泉) is a hot spring located in Hoshimata, [[:en:Tsumagoi|Tsumagoi Village]], [[:en:Agatsuma District, Gunma|Agatsuma District]], [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]], within the [[:en:Jōshin'etsu-kōgen National Park|Joshinetsu Kogen National Park]], at an elevation of 1,800 m. It is one of the highest hot springs in Japan that can be reached year‑round by private automobile, along with {{ill|Daku River Onsen|ja|濁河温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daku River Onsen|1=Nigorigo Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566108}} in [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]]’s [[:en:Gero, Gifu|Gero City]]. Nearby are three explosive crater lakes: Yubatake, Karagama, and Mizukama.
== Water Quality == * Acidic sulfur spring ({{ill|Hydrogen Sulfide Spring|ja|硫化水素泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hydrogen Sulfide Spring|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 硫黄泉|1=Sulfur Spring|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11587679}}) : The water is characterized by its milky white or yellowish appearance and contains the highest sulfur content of any hot spring in Japan. It has a discharge of 3,750 L per minute.
== Onsen Town == [[File:Manza ski resort Mt.Asahi 20080219.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Manza Onsen Ski Resort|ja|万座温泉スキー場|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Onsen Ski Resort|1=Manza Onsen Ski Resort|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353840}}]] Located on the slopes of [[:en:Mount Kusatsu-Shirane|Kusatsu Shirane]], Manza Onsen was once a thriving hot spring resort ({{ill|Yūji|ja|湯治場|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yūji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 湯治|1=thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}}) from spring through autumn and is now renowned as a ski destination in winter. The high temperature of its sources causes a continuous plume of steam, and the source areas display a hellish appearance with a pervasive sulfur odor. Hot spring sources emerge abundantly along the riverbed<ref name="noguchi">[[:en:Ill|Fuyuhito Noguchi|ja|野口冬人]]『湯治の宿』, Yomiuri Travel Publishing</ref>. A notable vent known as "Karabuki" near the outskirts of the central area is a local specialty (though its steam output has diminished since around 2012). The gently sloping mountainside is dotted with inns and hotels, with the {{ill|Manza Onsen Ski Resort|ja|万座温泉スキー場|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Onsen Ski Resort|1=Manza Onsen Ski Resort|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353840}} situated at the rear. Because the area is within a national park, facility expansions or renovations are limited, resulting in many aging structures. Some accommodations have closed or been absorbed by others; as of 2019, only 7 inns and hotels continue independent operations for individual guests. There are also lodging options with self-catering facilities for long-term hot spring therapy, though no convenience stores exist in the area apart from small shops attached to the accommodations. Additionally, the Manza Onsen Ski Resort was featured as a filming location for the movie [[Take Me Skiing]]. There are over 10 hot spring sources, including Yubatake, Banna no Yu, Daiku Yu, Tetsuyu, and Hōsei no Yu.
== History == The period of initial development is unclear; however, Yayoi pottery has been excavated in the vicinity. Legend has it that [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] exorcised demons here, and records indicate that samurai visited for hot spring therapy during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}. Although it operated as a hot spring resort during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], it did not flourish until after World War II, when the {{ill|Seibu Group|ja|西武グループ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seibu Group|1=Seibu Group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628875}} undertook resort development and transportation improved.
On February 22, 1983, the old building of Manza Onsen Hotel (Nisshinkan) caught fire, completely burning an area of 1,000 m². Four ski guests sustained minor injuries. According to the reports of the {{ill|Naganohara Police Station|ja|長野原警察署|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naganohara Police Station|1=Naganohara Police Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11654145}} and the {{ill|Agatsuma Western Regional Fire Department|ja|吾妻広域西部消防署|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Agatsuma Western Regional Fire Department|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, although 135 guests were staying in the old building, the deep snow enabled most to escape by jumping from the windows, preventing a far worse tragedy<ref name="mainichi">[[:en:Ill|Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞]] Evening Edition, February 23, 1983</ref>.
In 2004, during the hot spring misrepresentation scandal, a ryokan was reported to have displayed descriptions on its website suggesting hot spring usage despite not drawing any; subsequent corrections were made. As of 2019, the management of the affected facility has changed.
== Access == * Railway ({{ill|JR East|ja|JR東日本|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR East|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東日本旅客鉄道|1=East Japan Railway Company|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q499071}}) : Approximately 40 minutes by {{ill|Seibu Sightseeing Bus|ja|西武観光バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seibu Sightseeing Bus|1=Seibu Kanko Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628934}} from [[:en:Manza-Kazawaguchi Station|Manza-Kashizawa-guchi Station]] on the [[:en:Agatsuma Line|Agatsuma Line]]. : Approximately 90 minutes by Seibu Sightseeing Bus (via Manza-Kashizawa-guchi Station) from [[:en:Karuizawa Station|Karuizawa Station]] on the [[:en:Hokuriku Shinkansen|Hokuriku Shinkansen]]. * There are three bus stops in the Manza Onsen area: "Manza Onsen" (near the entrance), the central "Manza Prince Hotel" bus stop, and the "Manza Onsen Ue" bus stop. * Automobile: : The only road available year‑round is the paid {{ill|Manza Highway|ja|万座ハイウェー|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Highway|1=Manza highway|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353835}}, about 22 km from Mihara in Tsumagoi Village. : From spring to autumn, [[:en:Japan National Route 292|National Route 292]] (Shiga‑Kusatsu Kogen Route) is available (it is closed in winter). Note that due to the eruption alert level for [[:en:Mount Kusatsu-Shirane|Kusatsu Shirane]], the section between the (Kusatsu) {{ill|Sesshōgahara|ja|殺生河原|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sesshōgahara|1=Sesshōgawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q105410918}} parking area and the Manza Sansaji intersection may be closed. As of April 2019, the eruption alert level is 2; although normally it would be closed all day, an exception allows passage during daytime only (bicycles, motorcycles, and convertibles are not permitted; weather conditions such as fog may also cause closures).

[[File:Kusatsu-Shiranesan01s5s4272.jpg|thumb|image]] '''Manza Onsen''' (万座温泉) is a hot spring located in Hoshimata, [[:en:Tsumagoi|Tsumagoi Village]], [[:en:Agatsuma District, Gunma|Agatsuma District]], [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]], within the [[:en:Jōshin'etsu-kōgen National Park|Joshinetsu Kogen National Park]], at an elevation of 1,800 m. It is one of the highest hot springs in Japan that can be reached year‑round by private automobile, along with {{ill|Daku River Onsen|ja|濁河温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daku River Onsen|1=Nigorigo Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566108}} in [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]]’s [[:en:Gero, Gifu|Gero City]]. Nearby are three explosive crater lakes: Yubatake, Karagama, and Mizukama.

It has a discharge of 3,750 L per minute.
== Onsen Town == [[File:Manza ski resort Mt.Asahi 20080219.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Manza Onsen Ski Resort|ja|万座温泉スキー場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Onsen Ski Resort|1=Manza Onsen Ski Resort|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353840}}]] Located on the slopes of [[:en:Mount Kusatsu-Shirane|Kusatsu Shirane]], Manza Onsen was once a thriving hot spring resort ({{ill|Yūji|ja|湯治場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yūji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 湯治|1=thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}}) from spring through autumn and is now renowned as a ski destination in winter. The high temperature of its sources causes a continuous plume of steam, and the source areas display a hellish appearance with a pervasive sulfur odor. Hot spring sources emerge abundantly along the riverbed<ref name="noguchi">[[:en:Ill|Fuyuhito Noguchi|ja|野口冬人]]『湯治の宿』, Yomiuri Travel Publishing</ref>. A notable vent known as "Karabuki" near the outskirts of the central area is a local specialty (though its steam output has diminished since around 2012). The gently sloping mountainside is dotted with inns and hotels, with the {{ill|Manza Onsen Ski Resort|ja|万座温泉スキー場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Onsen Ski Resort|1=Manza Onsen Ski Resort|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353840}} situated at the rear. Because the area is within a national park, facility expansions or renovations are limited, resulting in many aging structures. Some accommodations have closed or been absorbed by others; as of 2019, only 7 inns and hotels continue independent operations for individual guests. There are also lodging options with self-catering facilities for long-term hot spring therapy, though no convenience stores exist in the area apart from small shops attached to the accommodations. Additionally, the Manza Onsen Ski Resort was featured as a filming location for the movie [[Take Me Skiing]]. There are over 10 hot spring sources, including Yubatake, Banna no Yu, Daiku Yu, Tetsuyu, and Hōsei no Yu.

Legend has it that [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] exorcised demons here, and records indicate that samurai visited for hot spring therapy during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}. Although it operated as a hot spring resort during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], it did not flourish until after World War II, when the {{ill|Seibu Group|ja|西武グループ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seibu Group|1=Seibu Group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628875}} undertook resort development and transportation improved.
On February 22, 1983, the old building of Manza Onsen Hotel (Nisshinkan) caught fire, completely burning an area of 1,000 m². Four ski guests sustained minor injuries. According to the reports of the {{ill|Naganohara Police Station|ja|長野原警察署|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naganohara Police Station|1=Naganohara Police Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11654145}} and the {{ill|Agatsuma Western Regional Fire Department|ja|吾妻広域西部消防署|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Agatsuma Western Regional Fire Department|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, although 135 guests were staying in the old building, the deep snow enabled most to escape by jumping from the windows, preventing a far worse tragedy<ref name="mainichi">[[:en:Ill|Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞]] Evening Edition, February 23, 1983</ref>.
In 2004, during the hot spring misrepresentation scandal, a ryokan was reported to have displayed descriptions on its website suggesting hot spring usage despite not drawing any; subsequent corrections were made. As of 2019, the management of the affected facility has changed.
== Access == * Railway ({{ill|JR East|ja|JR東日本|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR East|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東日本旅客鉄道|1=East Japan Railway Company|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q499071}}) : Approximately 40 minutes by {{ill|Seibu Sightseeing Bus|ja|西武観光バス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seibu Sightseeing Bus|1=Seibu Kanko Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628934}} from [[:en:Manza-Kazawaguchi Station|Manza-Kashizawa-guchi Station]] on the [[:en:Agatsuma Line|Agatsuma Line]]. : Approximately 90 minutes by Seibu Sightseeing Bus (via Manza-Kashizawa-guchi Station) from [[:en:Karuizawa Station|Karuizawa Station]] on the [[:en:Hokuriku Shinkansen|Hokuriku Shinkansen]]. * There are three bus stops in the Manza Onsen area: "Manza Onsen" (near the entrance), the central "Manza Prince Hotel" bus stop, and the "Manza Onsen Ue" bus stop. * Automobile: : The only road available year‑round is the paid {{ill|Manza Highway|ja|万座ハイウェー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Highway|1=Manza highway|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353835}}, about 22 km from Mihara in Tsumagoi Village. : From spring to autumn, [[:en:Japan National Route 292|National Route 292]] (Shiga‑Kusatsu Kogen Route) is available (it is closed in winter). Note that due to the eruption alert level for [[:en:Mount Kusatsu-Shirane|Kusatsu Shirane]], the section between the (Kusatsu) {{ill|Sesshōgahara|ja|殺生河原|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sesshōgahara|1=Sesshōgawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q105410918}} parking area and the Manza Sansaji intersection may be closed. As of April 2019, the eruption alert level is 2; although normally it would be closed all day, an exception allows passage during daytime only (bicycles, motorcycles, and convertibles are not permitted; weather conditions such as fog may also cause closures).

[[File:Kusatsu-Shiranesan01s5s4272.jpg|thumb|image]] '''Manza Onsen''' (万座温泉) is a hot spring located in Hoshimata, [[:en:Tsumagoi|Tsumagoi Village]], [[:en:Agatsuma District, Gunma|Agatsuma District]], [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]], within the [[:en:Jōshin'etsu-kōgen National Park|Joshinetsu Kogen National Park]], at an elevation of 1,800 m. It is one of the highest hot springs in Japan that can be reached year‑round by private automobile, along with {{ill|Daku River Onsen|ja|濁河温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daku River Onsen|1=Nigorigo Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566108}} in [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]]’s [[:en:Gero, Gifu|Gero City]]. Nearby are three explosive crater lakes: Yubatake, Karagama, and Mizukama.
== Water Quality == * Acidic sulfur spring ({{ill|Hydrogen Sulfide Spring|ja|硫化水素泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hydrogen Sulfide Spring|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 硫黄泉|1=Sulfur Spring|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11587679}}) : The water is characterized by its milky white or yellowish appearance and contains the highest sulfur content of any hot spring in Japan. It has a discharge of 3,750 L per minute.
== Onsen Town == [[File:Manza ski resort Mt.Asahi 20080219.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Manza Onsen Ski Resort|ja|万座温泉スキー場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Onsen Ski Resort|1=Manza Onsen Ski Resort|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353840}}]] Located on the slopes of [[:en:Mount Kusatsu-Shirane|Kusatsu Shirane]], Manza Onsen was once a thriving hot spring resort ({{ill|Yūji|ja|湯治場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yūji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 湯治|1=thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}}) from spring through autumn and is now renowned as a ski destination in winter. The high temperature of its sources causes a continuous plume of steam, and the source areas display a hellish appearance with a pervasive sulfur odor. Hot spring sources emerge abundantly along the riverbed<ref name="noguchi">[[:en:Ill|Fuyuhito Noguchi|ja|野口冬人]]『湯治の宿』, Yomiuri Travel Publishing</ref>. A notable vent known as "Karabuki" near the outskirts of the central area is a local specialty (though its steam output has diminished since around 2012). The gently sloping mountainside is dotted with inns and hotels, with the {{ill|Manza Onsen Ski Resort|ja|万座温泉スキー場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Onsen Ski Resort|1=Manza Onsen Ski Resort|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353840}} situated at the rear. Because the area is within a national park, facility expansions or renovations are limited, resulting in many aging structures. Some accommodations have closed or been absorbed by others; as of 2019, only 7 inns and hotels continue independent operations for individual guests. There are also lodging options with self-catering facilities for long-term hot spring therapy, though no convenience stores exist in the area apart from small shops attached to the accommodations. Additionally, the Manza Onsen Ski Resort was featured as a filming location for the movie [[Take Me Skiing]]. There are over 10 hot spring sources, including Yubatake, Banna no Yu, Daiku Yu, Tetsuyu, and Hōsei no Yu.
== History == The period of initial development is unclear; however, Yayoi pottery has been excavated in the vicinity. Legend has it that [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] exorcised demons here, and records indicate that samurai visited for hot spring therapy during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}. Although it operated as a hot spring resort during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], it did not flourish until after World War II, when the {{ill|Seibu Group|ja|西武グループ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seibu Group|1=Seibu Group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628875}} undertook resort development and transportation improved.
On February 22, 1983, the old building of Manza Onsen Hotel (Nisshinkan) caught fire, completely burning an area of 1,000 m². Four ski guests sustained minor injuries. According to the reports of the {{ill|Naganohara Police Station|ja|長野原警察署|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naganohara Police Station|1=Naganohara Police Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11654145}} and the {{ill|Agatsuma Western Regional Fire Department|ja|吾妻広域西部消防署|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Agatsuma Western Regional Fire Department|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, although 135 guests were staying in the old building, the deep snow enabled most to escape by jumping from the windows, preventing a far worse tragedy<ref name="mainichi">[[:en:Ill|Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞]] Evening Edition, February 23, 1983</ref>.
In 2004, during the hot spring misrepresentation scandal, a ryokan was reported to have displayed descriptions on its website suggesting hot spring usage despite not drawing any; subsequent corrections were made. As of 2019, the management of the affected facility has changed.
== Access == * Railway ({{ill|JR East|ja|JR東日本|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR East|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東日本旅客鉄道|1=East Japan Railway Company|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q499071}}) : Approximately 40 minutes by {{ill|Seibu Sightseeing Bus|ja|西武観光バス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seibu Sightseeing Bus|1=Seibu Kanko Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628934}} from [[:en:Manza-Kazawaguchi Station|Manza-Kashizawa-guchi Station]] on the [[:en:Agatsuma Line|Agatsuma Line]]. : Approximately 90 minutes by Seibu Sightseeing Bus (via Manza-Kashizawa-guchi Station) from [[:en:Karuizawa Station|Karuizawa Station]] on the [[:en:Hokuriku Shinkansen|Hokuriku Shinkansen]]. * There are three bus stops in the Manza Onsen area: "Manza Onsen" (near the entrance), the central "Manza Prince Hotel" bus stop, and the "Manza Onsen Ue" bus stop. * Automobile: : The only road available year‑round is the paid {{ill|Manza Highway|ja|万座ハイウェー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Highway|1=Manza highway|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353835}}, about 22 km from Mihara in Tsumagoi Village. : From spring to autumn, [[:en:Japan National Route 292|National Route 292]] (Shiga‑Kusatsu Kogen Route) is available (it is closed in winter). Note that due to the eruption alert level for [[:en:Mount Kusatsu-Shirane|Kusatsu Shirane]], the section between the (Kusatsu) {{ill|Sesshōgahara|ja|殺生河原|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sesshōgahara|1=Sesshōgawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q105410918}} parking area and the Manza Sansaji intersection may be closed. As of April 2019, the eruption alert level is 2; although normally it would be closed all day, an exception allows passage during daytime only (bicycles, motorcycles, and convertibles are not permitted; weather conditions such as fog may also cause closures).
== Footnotes ==
<references />
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Manza Onsen Ski Resort|ja|万座温泉スキー場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Onsen Ski Resort|1=Manza Onsen Ski Resort|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353840}} * {{ill|Manza Prince Hotel|ja|万座プリンスホテル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Prince Hotel|1=Manza Prince Hotel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353836}} * [[:en:Kusatsu Onsen|Kusatsu Onsen]] * {{ill|ii Yudana|ja|いい湯だな|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ii Yudana|1=ii Yudana|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11259008}} ({{ill|Duke Aces|ja|デューク・エイセス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Duke Aces|1=Duke Aces|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11320788}}) – Third set of lyrics
== External Links == * [http://www.manzaonsen.gr.jp/ Manza Onsen Tourism Association]

There are also lodging options with self-catering facilities for long-term hot spring therapy, though no convenience stores exist in the area apart from small shops attached to the accommodations. Additionally, the Manza Onsen Ski Resort was featured as a filming location for the movie [[Take Me Skiing]]. There are over 10 hot spring sources, including Yubatake, Banna no Yu, Daiku Yu, Tetsuyu, and Hōsei no Yu.
== History == The period of initial development is unclear; however, Yayoi pottery has been excavated in the vicinity. Legend has it that [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] exorcised demons here, and records indicate that samurai visited for hot spring therapy during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}. Although it operated as a hot spring resort during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], it did not flourish until after World War II, when the {{ill|Seibu Group|ja|西武グループ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seibu Group|1=Seibu Group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628875}} undertook resort development and transportation improved.
On February 22, 1983, the old building of Manza Onsen Hotel (Nisshinkan) caught fire, completely burning an area of 1,000 m². Four ski guests sustained minor injuries. According to the reports of the {{ill|Naganohara Police Station|ja|長野原警察署|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naganohara Police Station|1=Naganohara Police Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11654145}} and the {{ill|Agatsuma Western Regional Fire Department|ja|吾妻広域西部消防署|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Agatsuma Western Regional Fire Department|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, although 135 guests were staying in the old building, the deep snow enabled most to escape by jumping from the windows, preventing a far worse tragedy<ref name="mainichi">[[:en:Ill|Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞]] Evening Edition, February 23, 1983</ref>.
In 2004, during the hot spring misrepresentation scandal, a ryokan was reported to have displayed descriptions on its website suggesting hot spring usage despite not drawing any; subsequent corrections were made. As of 2019, the management of the affected facility has changed.
== Access == * Railway ({{ill|JR East|ja|JR東日本|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR East|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東日本旅客鉄道|1=East Japan Railway Company|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q499071}}) : Approximately 40 minutes by {{ill|Seibu Sightseeing Bus|ja|西武観光バス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seibu Sightseeing Bus|1=Seibu Kanko Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628934}} from [[:en:Manza-Kazawaguchi Station|Manza-Kashizawa-guchi Station]] on the [[:en:Agatsuma Line|Agatsuma Line]]. : Approximately 90 minutes by Seibu Sightseeing Bus (via Manza-Kashizawa-guchi Station) from [[:en:Karuizawa Station|Karuizawa Station]] on the [[:en:Hokuriku Shinkansen|Hokuriku Shinkansen]]. * There are three bus stops in the Manza Onsen area: "Manza Onsen" (near the entrance), the central "Manza Prince Hotel" bus stop, and the "Manza Onsen Ue" bus stop. * Automobile: : The only road available year‑round is the paid {{ill|Manza Highway|ja|万座ハイウェー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Highway|1=Manza highway|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353835}}, about 22 km from Mihara in Tsumagoi Village. : From spring to autumn, [[:en:Japan National Route 292|National Route 292]] (Shiga‑Kusatsu Kogen Route) is available (it is closed in winter). Note that due to the eruption alert level for [[:en:Mount Kusatsu-Shirane|Kusatsu Shirane]], the section between the (Kusatsu) {{ill|Sesshōgahara|ja|殺生河原|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sesshōgahara|1=Sesshōgawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q105410918}} parking area and the Manza Sansaji intersection may be closed. As of April 2019, the eruption alert level is 2; although normally it would be closed all day, an exception allows passage during daytime only (bicycles, motorcycles, and convertibles are not permitted; weather conditions such as fog may also cause closures).
