<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Manza Onsen]][[ja:万座温泉]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Manza Onsen]][[ja:万座温泉]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Manza Onsen]][[ja:万座温泉]]
{{Referencesの明記|date=March 2017}}{{infobox Onsen | 名称 = Manza Onsen | native_name = 万座温泉 | image = Manza Onsen Tsumagoi Gunma01bs4272.jpg | コメント = Manza-yama and Manza Onsen | 所在地 = {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture}} {{ill|Agatsuma District, Gunma|en|Agatsuma District, Gunma|WD=Q642394|lt=Agatsuma District}} {{ill|Tsumagoi|en|Tsumagoi|WD=Q1203321|lt=Tsumagoi Village}} 干俣 | 緯度度 = 36 | 緯度分 = 38 | 緯度秒 = 5.1 | 経度度 = 138 | 経度分 = 30 | 経度秒 = 31.7 | 交通アクセス = ''See the [[#Access|Access]] section.'' | 泉質 = Acidic sulfur spring | 泉温 = Approximately 80°C | 湧出量 = Approximately 5,400,000 {{ill|Litre|en|Litre|WD=Q11582|lt=L}}/day | pH = | 液性の分類 = | 浸透圧の分類 = | 宿泊施設数 = 9 | 総収容人員数 = | 年間浴客数 = | 統計年度 = | External links = http://www.manzaonsen.gr.jp/ | 特記事項 = }}
[[File:Kusatsu-Shiranesan01s5s4272.jpg|thumb|image]] '''Manza Onsen''' (万座温泉) is a hot spring located in Hoshimata, {{ill|Tsumagoi|en|Tsumagoi|WD=Q1203321|lt=Tsumagoi Village}}, {{ill|Agatsuma District, Gunma|en|Agatsuma District, Gunma|WD=Q642394|lt=Agatsuma District}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture}}, within the {{ill|Jōshin'etsu-kōgen National Park|en|Jōshin'etsu-kōgen National Park|WD=Q1155306|lt=Joshinetsu Kogen National Park}}, at an elevation of 1,800 m. It is one of the highest hot springs in Japan that can be reached year‑round by private automobile, along with {{ill|Daku River Onsen|ja|濁河温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daku River Onsen|1=Nigorigo Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566108}} in {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|en|Gifu Prefecture|WD=Q131277|lt=Gifu Prefecture}}’s {{ill|Gero, Gifu|en|Gero, Gifu|WD=Q819639|lt=Gero City}}. Nearby are three explosive crater lakes: Yubatake, Karagama, and Mizukama.
== Water Quality == * Acidic sulfur spring ({{ill|Hydrogen Sulfide Spring|ja|硫化水素泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hydrogen Sulfide Spring|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 硫黄泉|1=Sulfur Spring|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11587679}}) : The water is characterized by its milky white or yellowish appearance and contains the highest sulfur content of any hot spring in Japan. It has a discharge of 3,750 L per minute.
== Onsen Town == [[File:Manza ski resort Mt.Asahi 20080219.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Manza Onsen Ski Resort|ja|万座温泉スキー場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Onsen Ski Resort|1=Manza Onsen Ski Resort|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353840}}]] Located on the slopes of {{ill|Mount Kusatsu-Shirane|en|Mount Kusatsu-Shirane|WD=Q279915|lt=Kusatsu Shirane}}, Manza Onsen was once a thriving hot spring resort ({{ill|Yūji|ja|湯治場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yūji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 湯治|1=thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}}) from spring through autumn and is now renowned as a ski destination in winter. The high temperature of its sources causes a continuous plume of steam, and the source areas display a hellish appearance with a pervasive sulfur odor. Hot spring sources emerge abundantly along the riverbed<ref name="noguchi">{{ill|Ill|en|Ill|WD=Q227554|lt=Fuyuhito Noguchi|ja|野口冬人}}『湯治の宿』, Yomiuri Travel Publishing</ref>. A notable vent known as "Karabuki" near the outskirts of the central area is a local specialty (though its steam output has diminished since around 2012). The gently sloping mountainside is dotted with inns and hotels, with the {{ill|Manza Onsen Ski Resort|ja|万座温泉スキー場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Onsen Ski Resort|1=Manza Onsen Ski Resort|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353840}} situated at the rear. Because the area is within a national park, facility expansions or renovations are limited, resulting in many aging structures. Some accommodations have closed or been absorbed by others; as of 2019, only 7 inns and hotels continue independent operations for individual guests. There are also lodging options with self-catering facilities for long-term hot spring therapy, though no convenience stores exist in the area apart from small shops attached to the accommodations. Additionally, the Manza Onsen Ski Resort was featured as a filming location for the movie [[Take Me Skiing]]. There are over 10 hot spring sources, including Yubatake, Banna no Yu, Daiku Yu, Tetsuyu, and Hōsei no Yu.
== History == The period of initial development is unclear; however, Yayoi pottery has been excavated in the vicinity. Legend has it that {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|en|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|WD=Q941979|lt=Sakanoue no Tamuramaro}} exorcised demons here, and records indicate that samurai visited for hot spring therapy during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}. Although it operated as a hot spring resort during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, it did not flourish until after World War II, when the {{ill|Seibu Group|ja|西武グループ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seibu Group|1=Seibu Group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628875}} undertook resort development and transportation improved.
On February 22, 1983, the old building of Manza Onsen Hotel (Nisshinkan) caught fire, completely burning an area of 1,000 m². Four ski guests sustained minor injuries. According to the reports of the {{ill|Naganohara Police Station|ja|長野原警察署|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naganohara Police Station|1=Naganohara Police Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11654145}} and the {{ill|Agatsuma Western Regional Fire Department|ja|吾妻広域西部消防署|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Agatsuma Western Regional Fire Department|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, although 135 guests were staying in the old building, the deep snow enabled most to escape by jumping from the windows, preventing a far worse tragedy<ref name="mainichi">{{ill|Ill|en|Ill|WD=Q227554|lt=Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞}} Evening Edition, February 23, 1983</ref>.
In 2004, during the hot spring misrepresentation scandal, a ryokan was reported to have displayed descriptions on its website suggesting hot spring usage despite not drawing any; subsequent corrections were made. As of 2019, the management of the affected facility has changed.
== Access == * Railway ({{ill|JR East|ja|JR東日本|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR East|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東日本旅客鉄道|1=East Japan Railway Company|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q499071}}) : Approximately 40 minutes by {{ill|Seibu Sightseeing Bus|ja|西武観光バス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seibu Sightseeing Bus|1=Seibu Kanko Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628934}} from {{ill|Manza-Kazawaguchi Station|en|Manza-Kazawaguchi Station|WD=Q6753421|lt=Manza-Kashizawa-guchi Station}} on the {{ill|Agatsuma Line|en|Agatsuma Line|WD=Q391568|lt=Agatsuma Line}}. : Approximately 90 minutes by Seibu Sightseeing Bus (via Manza-Kashizawa-guchi Station) from {{ill|Karuizawa Station|en|Karuizawa Station|WD=Q1064215|lt=Karuizawa Station}} on the {{ill|Hokuriku Shinkansen|en|Hokuriku Shinkansen|WD=Q1037409|lt=Hokuriku Shinkansen}}. * There are three bus stops in the Manza Onsen area: "Manza Onsen" (near the entrance), the central "Manza Prince Hotel" bus stop, and the "Manza Onsen Ue" bus stop. * Automobile: : The only road available year‑round is the paid {{ill|Manza Highway|ja|万座ハイウェー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Highway|1=Manza highway|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353835}}, about 22 km from Mihara in Tsumagoi Village. : From spring to autumn, {{ill|Japan National Route 292|en|Japan National Route 292|WD=Q11421437|lt=National Route 292}} (Shiga‑Kusatsu Kogen Route) is available (it is closed in winter). Note that due to the eruption alert level for {{ill|Mount Kusatsu-Shirane|en|Mount Kusatsu-Shirane|WD=Q279915|lt=Kusatsu Shirane}}, the section between the (Kusatsu) {{ill|Sesshōgahara|ja|殺生河原|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sesshōgahara|1=Sesshōgawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q105410918}} parking area and the Manza Sansaji intersection may be closed. As of April 2019, the eruption alert level is 2; although normally it would be closed all day, an exception allows passage during daytime only (bicycles, motorcycles, and convertibles are not permitted; weather conditions such as fog may also cause closures).
== Footnotes ==

== Related Articles == * {{ill|Manza Onsen Ski Resort|ja|万座温泉スキー場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Onsen Ski Resort|1=Manza Onsen Ski Resort|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353840}} * {{ill|Manza Prince Hotel|ja|万座プリンスホテル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Prince Hotel|1=Manza Prince Hotel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353836}} * {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|en|Kusatsu Onsen|WD=Q3137477|lt=Kusatsu Onsen}} * {{ill|ii Yudana|ja|いい湯だな|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ii Yudana|1=ii Yudana|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11259008}} ({{ill|Duke Aces|ja|デューク・エイセス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Duke Aces|1=Duke Aces|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11320788}}) – Third set of lyrics

According to the reports of the {{ill|Naganohara Police Station|ja|長野原警察署|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naganohara Police Station|1=Naganohara Police Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11654145}} and the {{ill|Agatsuma Western Regional Fire Department|ja|吾妻広域西部消防署|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Agatsuma Western Regional Fire Department|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, although 135 guests were staying in the old building, the deep snow enabled most to escape by jumping from the windows, preventing a far worse tragedy<ref name="mainichi">{{ill|Ill|en|Ill|WD=Q227554|lt=Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞}} Evening Edition, February 23, 1983</ref>.
In 2004, during the hot spring misrepresentation scandal, a ryokan was reported to have displayed descriptions on its website suggesting hot spring usage despite not drawing any; subsequent corrections were made. As of 2019, the management of the affected facility has changed.
== Access == * Railway ({{ill|JR East|ja|JR東日本|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR East|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東日本旅客鉄道|1=East Japan Railway Company|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q499071}}) : Approximately 40 minutes by {{ill|Seibu Sightseeing Bus|ja|西武観光バス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seibu Sightseeing Bus|1=Seibu Kanko Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628934}} from {{ill|Manza-Kazawaguchi Station|en|Manza-Kazawaguchi Station|WD=Q6753421|lt=Manza-Kashizawa-guchi Station}} on the {{ill|Agatsuma Line|en|Agatsuma Line|WD=Q391568|lt=Agatsuma Line}}. : Approximately 90 minutes by Seibu Sightseeing Bus (via Manza-Kashizawa-guchi Station) from {{ill|Karuizawa Station|en|Karuizawa Station|WD=Q1064215|lt=Karuizawa Station}} on the {{ill|Hokuriku Shinkansen|en|Hokuriku Shinkansen|WD=Q1037409|lt=Hokuriku Shinkansen}}. * There are three bus stops in the Manza Onsen area: "Manza Onsen" (near the entrance), the central "Manza Prince Hotel" bus stop, and the "Manza Onsen Ue" bus stop. * Automobile: : The only road available year‑round is the paid {{ill|Manza Highway|ja|万座ハイウェー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Highway|1=Manza highway|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353835}}, about 22 km from Mihara in Tsumagoi Village. : From spring to autumn, {{ill|Japan National Route 292|en|Japan National Route 292|WD=Q11421437|lt=National Route 292}} (Shiga‑Kusatsu Kogen Route) is available (it is closed in winter). Note that due to the eruption alert level for {{ill|Mount Kusatsu-Shirane|en|Mount Kusatsu-Shirane|WD=Q279915|lt=Kusatsu Shirane}}, the section between the (Kusatsu) {{ill|Sesshōgahara|ja|殺生河原|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sesshōgahara|1=Sesshōgawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q105410918}} parking area and the Manza Sansaji intersection may be closed. As of April 2019, the eruption alert level is 2; although normally it would be closed all day, an exception allows passage during daytime only (bicycles, motorcycles, and convertibles are not permitted; weather conditions such as fog may also cause closures).

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Manza Onsen]][[ja:万座温泉]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Manza Onsen]][[ja:万座温泉]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Manza Onsen]][[ja:万座温泉]]
{{Referencesの明記|date=March 2017}}{{infobox Onsen | 名称 = Manza Onsen | native_name = 万座温泉 | image = Manza Onsen Tsumagoi Gunma01bs4272.jpg | コメント = Manza-yama and Manza Onsen | 所在地 = {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture}} {{ill|Agatsuma District, Gunma|en|Agatsuma District, Gunma|WD=Q642394|lt=Agatsuma District}} {{ill|Tsumagoi|en|Tsumagoi|WD=Q1203321|lt=Tsumagoi Village}} 干俣 | 緯度度 = 36 | 緯度分 = 38 | 緯度秒 = 5.1 | 経度度 = 138 | 経度分 = 30 | 経度秒 = 31.7 | 交通アクセス = ''See the [[#Access|Access]] section.'' | 泉質 = Acidic sulfur spring | 泉温 = Approximately 80°C | 湧出量 = Approximately 5,400,000 {{ill|Litre|en|Litre|WD=Q11582|lt=L}}/day | pH = | 液性の分類 = | 浸透圧の分類 = | 宿泊施設数 = 9 | 総収容人員数 = | 年間浴客数 = | 統計年度 = | External links = http://www.manzaonsen.gr.jp/ | 特記事項 = }}
[[File:Kusatsu-Shiranesan01s5s4272.jpg|thumb|image]] '''Manza Onsen''' (万座温泉) is a hot spring located in Hoshimata, {{ill|Tsumagoi|en|Tsumagoi|WD=Q1203321|lt=Tsumagoi Village}}, {{ill|Agatsuma District, Gunma|en|Agatsuma District, Gunma|WD=Q642394|lt=Agatsuma District}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture}}, within the {{ill|Jōshin'etsu-kōgen National Park|en|Jōshin'etsu-kōgen National Park|WD=Q1155306|lt=Joshinetsu Kogen National Park}}, at an elevation of 1,800 m. It is one of the highest hot springs in Japan that can be reached year-round by private automobile, along with {{ill|Daku River Onsen|ja|濁河温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daku River Onsen|1=Nigorigo Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566108}} in {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|en|Gifu Prefecture|WD=Q131277|lt=Gifu Prefecture}}’s {{ill|Gero, Gifu|en|Gero, Gifu|WD=Q819639|lt=Gero City}}. Nearby are three explosive crater lakes: Yubatake, Karagama, and Mizukama.
== Water Quality == * Acidic sulfur spring ({{ill|Hydrogen Sulfide Spring|ja|硫化水素泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hydrogen Sulfide Spring|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 硫黄泉|1=Sulfur Spring|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11587679}}) : The water is characterized by its milky white or yellowish appearance and contains the highest sulfur content of any hot spring in Japan. It has a discharge of 3,750 L per minute.
== Onsen Town == [[File:Manza ski resort Mt.Asahi 20080219.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Manza Onsen Ski Resort|ja|万座温泉スキー場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Onsen Ski Resort|1=Manza Onsen Ski Resort|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353840}}]] Located on the slopes of {{ill|Mount Kusatsu-Shirane|en|Mount Kusatsu-Shirane|WD=Q279915|lt=Kusatsu Shirane}}, Manza Onsen was once a thriving hot spring resort ({{ill|Yūji|ja|湯治場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yūji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 湯治|1=thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}}) from spring through autumn and is now renowned as a ski destination in winter. The high temperature of its sources causes a continuous plume of steam, and the source areas display a hellish appearance with a pervasive sulfur odor. Hot spring sources emerge abundantly along the riverbed<ref name="noguchi">{{ill|Ill|en|Ill|WD=Q227554|lt=Fuyuhito Noguchi|ja|野口冬人}}『湯治の宿』, Yomiuri Travel Publishing</ref>. A notable vent known as "Karabuki" near the outskirts of the central area is a local specialty (though its steam output has diminished since around 2012). The gently sloping mountainside is dotted with inns and hotels, with the {{ill|Manza Onsen Ski Resort|ja|万座温泉スキー場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Onsen Ski Resort|1=Manza Onsen Ski Resort|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353840}} situated at the rear. Because the area is within a national park, facility expansions or renovations are limited, resulting in many aging structures. Some accommodations have closed or been absorbed by others; as of 2019, only 7 inns and hotels continue independent operations for individual guests. There are also lodging options with self-catering facilities for long-term hot spring therapy, though no convenience stores exist in the area apart from small shops attached to the accommodations. Additionally, the Manza Onsen Ski Resort was featured as a filming location for the movie [[Take Me Skiing]]. There are over 10 hot spring sources, including Yubatake, Banna no Yu, Daiku Yu, Tetsuyu, and Hōsei no Yu.
== History == The period of initial development is unclear; however, Yayoi pottery has been excavated in the vicinity. Legend has it that {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|en|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|WD=Q941979|lt=Sakanoue no Tamuramaro}} exorcised demons here, and records indicate that samurai visited for hot spring therapy during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}. Although it operated as a hot spring resort during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, it did not flourish until after World War II, when the {{ill|Seibu Group|ja|西武グループ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seibu Group|1=Seibu Group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628875}} undertook resort development and transportation improved.
On February 22, 1983, the old building of Manza Onsen Hotel (Nisshinkan) caught fire, completely burning an area of 1,000 m². Four ski guests sustained minor injuries. According to the reports of the {{ill|Naganohara Police Station|ja|長野原警察署|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naganohara Police Station|1=Naganohara Police Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11654145}} and the {{ill|Agatsuma Western Regional Fire Department|ja|吾妻広域西部消防署|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Agatsuma Western Regional Fire Department|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, although 135 guests were staying in the old building, the deep snow enabled most to escape by jumping from the windows, preventing a far worse tragedy<ref name="mainichi">{{ill|Ill|en|Ill|WD=Q227554|lt=Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞}} Evening Edition, February 23, 1983</ref>.
In 2004, during the hot spring misrepresentation scandal, a ryokan was reported to have displayed descriptions on its website suggesting hot spring usage despite not drawing any; subsequent corrections were made. As of 2019, the management of the affected facility has changed.
== Access == * Railway ({{ill|JR East|ja|JR東日本|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR East|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東日本旅客鉄道|1=East Japan Railway Company|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q499071}}) : Approximately 40 minutes by {{ill|Seibu Sightseeing Bus|ja|西武観光バス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seibu Sightseeing Bus|1=Seibu Kanko Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628934}} from {{ill|Manza-Kazawaguchi Station|en|Manza-Kazawaguchi Station|WD=Q6753421|lt=Manza-Kashizawa-guchi Station}} on the {{ill|Agatsuma Line|en|Agatsuma Line|WD=Q391568|lt=Agatsuma Line}}. : Approximately 90 minutes by Seibu Sightseeing Bus (via Manza-Kashizawa-guchi Station) from {{ill|Karuizawa Station|en|Karuizawa Station|WD=Q1064215|lt=Karuizawa Station}} on the {{ill|Hokuriku Shinkansen|en|Hokuriku Shinkansen|WD=Q1037409|lt=Hokuriku Shinkansen}}. * There are three bus stops in the Manza Onsen area: "Manza Onsen" (near the entrance), the central "Manza Prince Hotel" bus stop, and the "Manza Onsen Ue" bus stop. * Automobile: : The only road available year-round is the paid {{ill|Manza Highway|ja|万座ハイウェー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Highway|1=Manza highway|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353835}}, about 22 km from Mihara in Tsumagoi Village. : From spring to autumn, {{ill|Japan National Route 292|en|Japan National Route 292|WD=Q11421437|lt=National Route 292}} (Shiga-Kusatsu Kogen Route) is available (it is closed in winter). Note that due to the eruption alert level for {{ill|Mount Kusatsu-Shirane|en|Mount Kusatsu-Shirane|WD=Q279915|lt=Kusatsu Shirane}}, the section between the (Kusatsu) {{ill|Sesshōgahara|ja|殺生河原|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sesshōgahara|1=Sesshōgawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q105410918}} parking area and the Manza Sansaji intersection may be closed. As of April 2019, the eruption alert level is 2; although normally it would be closed all day, an exception allows passage during daytime only (bicycles, motorcycles, and convertibles are not permitted; weather conditions such as fog may also cause closures).
== Footnotes ==
<references />
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Manza Onsen Ski Resort|ja|万座温泉スキー場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Onsen Ski Resort|1=Manza Onsen Ski Resort|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353840}} * {{ill|Manza Prince Hotel|ja|万座プリンスホテル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Prince Hotel|1=Manza Prince Hotel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353836}} * {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|en|Kusatsu Onsen|WD=Q3137477|lt=Kusatsu Onsen}} * {{ill|ii Yudana|ja|いい湯だな|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ii Yudana|1=ii Yudana|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11259008}} ({{ill|Duke Aces|ja|デューク・エイセス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Duke Aces|1=Duke Aces|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11320788}}) – Third set of lyrics

According to the reports of the {{ill|Naganohara Police Station|ja|長野原警察署|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naganohara Police Station|1=Q11654145|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11654145}} and the {{ill|Agatsuma Western Regional Fire Department|ja|吾妻広域西部消防署|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Agatsuma Western Regional Fire Department|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, although 135 guests were staying in the old building, the deep snow enabled most to escape by jumping from the windows, preventing a far worse tragedy<ref name="mainichi">{{ill|Ill|en|Ill|WD=Q227554|lt=Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞|1=Ill}} Evening Edition, February 23, 1983</ref>.
In 2004, during the hot spring misrepresentation scandal, a ryokan was reported to have displayed descriptions on its website suggesting hot spring usage despite not drawing any; subsequent corrections were made. As of 2019, the management of the affected facility has changed.
== Access == * Railway ({{ill|JR East|ja|JR東日本|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR East|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東日本旅客鉄道|1=East Japan Railway Company|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q499071}}) : Approximately 40 minutes by {{ill|Seibu Sightseeing Bus|ja|西武観光バス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seibu Sightseeing Bus|1=Seibu Kanko Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628934}} from {{ill|Manza-Kazawaguchi Station|en|Manza-Kazawaguchi Station|WD=Q6753421|lt=Manza-Kashizawa-guchi Station|1=Manza-Kazawaguchi Station}} on the {{ill|Agatsuma Line|en|Agatsuma Line|WD=Q391568|lt=Agatsuma Line|1=Agatsuma Line}}. : Approximately 90 minutes by Seibu Sightseeing Bus (via Manza-Kashizawa-guchi Station) from {{ill|Karuizawa Station|en|Karuizawa Station|WD=Q1064215|lt=Karuizawa Station|1=Karuizawa Station}} on the {{ill|Hokuriku Shinkansen|en|Hokuriku Shinkansen|WD=Q1037409|lt=Hokuriku Shinkansen|1=Hokuriku Shinkansen}}. * There are three bus stops in the Manza Onsen area: "Manza Onsen" (near the entrance), the central "Manza Prince Hotel" bus stop, and the "Manza Onsen Ue" bus stop. * Automobile: : The only road available year-round is the paid {{ill|Manza Highway|ja|万座ハイウェー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Highway|1=Manza highway|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353835}}, about 22 km from Mihara in Tsumagoi Village. : From spring to autumn, {{ill|Japan National Route 292|en|Japan National Route 292|WD=Q11421437|lt=National Route 292|1=Japan National Route 292}} (Shiga-Kusatsu Kogen Route) is available (it is closed in winter). Note that due to the eruption alert level for {{ill|Mount Kusatsu-Shirane|en|Mount Kusatsu-Shirane|WD=Q279915|lt=Kusatsu Shirane|1=Mount Kusatsu-Shirane}}, the section between the (Kusatsu) {{ill|Sesshōgahara|ja|殺生河原|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sesshōgahara|1=Sesshōgawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q105410918}} parking area and the Manza Sansaji intersection may be closed. As of April 2019, the eruption alert level is 2; although normally it would be closed all day, an exception allows passage during daytime only (bicycles, motorcycles, and convertibles are not permitted; weather conditions such as fog may also cause closures).

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Manza Onsen]][[ja:万座温泉]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Manza Onsen]][[ja:万座温泉]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|commons:Category:Manza Onsen]][[ja:万座温泉]]
{{Referencesの明記|date=March 2017}}{{infobox Onsen | 名称 = Manza Onsen | native_name = 万座温泉 | image = Manza Onsen Tsumagoi Gunma01bs4272.jpg | コメント = Manza-yama and Manza Onsen | 所在地 = {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture|1=Gunma Prefecture}} {{ill|Agatsuma District, Gunma|en|Agatsuma District, Gunma|WD=Q642394|lt=Agatsuma District|1=Agatsuma District, Gunma}} {{ill|Tsumagoi|en|Tsumagoi|WD=Q1203321|lt=Tsumagoi Village|1=Tsumagoi}} 干俣 | 緯度度 = 36 | 緯度分 = 38 | 緯度秒 = 5.1 | 経度度 = 138 | 経度分 = 30 | 経度秒 = 31.7 | 交通アクセス = ''See the [[#Access|Access]] section.'' | 泉質 = Acidic sulfur spring | 泉温 = Approximately 80°C | 湧出量 = Approximately 5,400,000 {{ill|Litre|en|Litre|WD=Q11582|lt=L|1=Litre}}/day | pH = | 液性の分類 = | 浸透圧の分類 = | 宿泊施設数 = 9 | 総収容人員数 = | 年間浴客数 = | 統計年度 = | External links = http://www.manzaonsen.gr.jp/ | 特記事項 = }}
[[File:Kusatsu-Shiranesan01s5s4272.jpg|thumb|image]] '''Manza Onsen''' (万座温泉) is a hot spring located in Hoshimata, {{ill|Tsumagoi|en|Tsumagoi|WD=Q1203321|lt=Tsumagoi Village|1=Tsumagoi}}, {{ill|Agatsuma District, Gunma|en|Agatsuma District, Gunma|WD=Q642394|lt=Agatsuma District|1=Agatsuma District, Gunma}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture|1=Gunma Prefecture}}, within the {{ill|Jōshin'etsu-kōgen National Park|en|Jōshin'etsu-kōgen National Park|WD=Q1155306|lt=Joshinetsu Kogen National Park|1=Jōshin'etsu-kōgen National Park}}, at an elevation of 1,800 m. It is one of the highest hot springs in Japan that can be reached year-round by private automobile, along with {{ill|Daku River Onsen|ja|濁河温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daku River Onsen|1=Nigorigo Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566108}} in {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|en|Gifu Prefecture|WD=Q131277|lt=Gifu Prefecture|1=Gifu Prefecture}}’s {{ill|Gero, Gifu|en|Gero, Gifu|WD=Q819639|lt=Gero City|1=Gero, Gifu}}. Nearby are three explosive crater lakes: Yubatake, Karagama, and Mizukama.
== Water Quality == * Acidic sulfur spring ({{ill|Hydrogen Sulfide Spring|ja|硫化水素泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hydrogen Sulfide Spring|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 硫黄泉|1=Sulfur spring|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11587679}}) : The water is characterized by its milky white or yellowish appearance and contains the highest sulfur content of any hot spring in Japan. It has a discharge of 3,750 L per minute.
== Onsen Town == [[File:Manza ski resort Mt.Asahi 20080219.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Manza Onsen Ski Resort|ja|万座温泉スキー場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Onsen Ski Resort|1=Manza Onsen Ski Resort|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353840}}]] Located on the slopes of {{ill|Mount Kusatsu-Shirane|en|Mount Kusatsu-Shirane|WD=Q279915|lt=Kusatsu Shirane|1=Mount Kusatsu-Shirane}}, Manza Onsen was once a thriving hot spring resort ({{ill|Yūji|ja|湯治場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yūji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 湯治|1=Thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}}) from spring through autumn and is now renowned as a ski destination in winter. The high temperature of its sources causes a continuous plume of steam, and the source areas display a hellish appearance with a pervasive sulfur odor. Hot spring sources emerge abundantly along the riverbed<ref name="noguchi">{{ill|Ill|en|Ill|WD=Q227554|lt=Fuyuhito Noguchi|ja|野口冬人|1=Ill}}『湯治の宿』, Yomiuri Travel Publishing</ref>. A notable vent known as "Karabuki" near the outskirts of the central area is a local specialty (though its steam output has diminished since around 2012). The gently sloping mountainside is dotted with inns and hotels, with the {{ill|Manza Onsen Ski Resort|ja|万座温泉スキー場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Onsen Ski Resort|1=Manza Onsen Ski Resort|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353840}} situated at the rear. Because the area is within a national park, facility expansions or renovations are limited, resulting in many aging structures. Some accommodations have closed or been absorbed by others; as of 2019, only 7 inns and hotels continue independent operations for individual guests. There are also lodging options with self-catering facilities for long-term hot spring therapy, though no convenience stores exist in the area apart from small shops attached to the accommodations. Additionally, the Manza Onsen Ski Resort was featured as a filming location for the movie [[Take Me Skiing]]. There are over 10 hot spring sources, including Yubatake, Banna no Yu, Daiku Yu, Tetsuyu, and Hōsei no Yu.
== History == The period of initial development is unclear; however, Yayoi pottery has been excavated in the vicinity. Legend has it that {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|en|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|WD=Q941979|lt=Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|1=Sakanoue no Tamuramaro}} exorcised demons here, and records indicate that samurai visited for hot spring therapy during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}. Although it operated as a hot spring resort during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, it did not flourish until after World War II, when the {{ill|Seibu Group|ja|西武グループ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seibu Group|1=Seibu Group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628875}} undertook resort development and transportation improved.
On February 22, 1983, the old building of Manza Onsen Hotel (Nisshinkan) caught fire, completely burning an area of 1,000 m². Four ski guests sustained minor injuries. According to the reports of the {{ill|Naganohara Police Station|ja|長野原警察署|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naganohara Police Station|1=Q11654145|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11654145}} and the {{ill|Agatsuma Western Regional Fire Department|ja|吾妻広域西部消防署|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Agatsuma Western Regional Fire Department|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, although 135 guests were staying in the old building, the deep snow enabled most to escape by jumping from the windows, preventing a far worse tragedy<ref name="mainichi">{{ill|Ill|en|Ill|WD=Q227554|lt=Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞|1=Ill}} Evening Edition, February 23, 1983</ref>.
In 2004, during the hot spring misrepresentation scandal, a ryokan was reported to have displayed descriptions on its website suggesting hot spring usage despite not drawing any; subsequent corrections were made. As of 2019, the management of the affected facility has changed.
== Access == * Railway ({{ill|JR East|ja|JR東日本|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR East|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東日本旅客鉄道|1=East Japan Railway Company|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q499071}}) : Approximately 40 minutes by {{ill|Seibu Sightseeing Bus|ja|西武観光バス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seibu Sightseeing Bus|1=Seibu Kanko Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628934}} from {{ill|Manza-Kazawaguchi Station|en|Manza-Kazawaguchi Station|WD=Q6753421|lt=Manza-Kashizawa-guchi Station|1=Manza-Kazawaguchi Station}} on the {{ill|Agatsuma Line|en|Agatsuma Line|WD=Q391568|lt=Agatsuma Line|1=Agatsuma Line}}. : Approximately 90 minutes by Seibu Sightseeing Bus (via Manza-Kashizawa-guchi Station) from {{ill|Karuizawa Station|en|Karuizawa Station|WD=Q1064215|lt=Karuizawa Station|1=Karuizawa Station}} on the {{ill|Hokuriku Shinkansen|en|Hokuriku Shinkansen|WD=Q1037409|lt=Hokuriku Shinkansen|1=Hokuriku Shinkansen}}. * There are three bus stops in the Manza Onsen area: "Manza Onsen" (near the entrance), the central "Manza Prince Hotel" bus stop, and the "Manza Onsen Ue" bus stop. * Automobile: : The only road available year-round is the paid {{ill|Manza Highway|ja|万座ハイウェー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Highway|1=Manza highway|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353835}}, about 22 km from Mihara in Tsumagoi Village. : From spring to autumn, {{ill|Japan National Route 292|en|Japan National Route 292|WD=Q11421437|lt=National Route 292|1=Japan National Route 292}} (Shiga-Kusatsu Kogen Route) is available (it is closed in winter). Note that due to the eruption alert level for {{ill|Mount Kusatsu-Shirane|en|Mount Kusatsu-Shirane|WD=Q279915|lt=Kusatsu Shirane|1=Mount Kusatsu-Shirane}}, the section between the (Kusatsu) {{ill|Sesshōgahara|ja|殺生河原|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sesshōgahara|1=Sesshōgawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q105410918}} parking area and the Manza Sansaji intersection may be closed. As of April 2019, the eruption alert level is 2; although normally it would be closed all day, an exception allows passage during daytime only (bicycles, motorcycles, and convertibles are not permitted; weather conditions such as fog may also cause closures).

On February 22, 1983, the old building of Manza Onsen Hotel (Nisshinkan) caught fire, completely burning an area of 1,000 m². Four ski guests sustained minor injuries. According to the reports of the {{ill|Naganohara Police Station|ja|長野原警察署|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naganohara Police Station|1=Q11654145|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11654145}} and the {{ill|Agatsuma Western Regional Fire Department|ja|吾妻広域西部消防署|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Agatsuma Western Regional Fire Department|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, although 135 guests were staying in the old building, the deep snow enabled most to escape by jumping from the windows, preventing a far worse tragedy<ref name="mainichi">{{ill|Ill|en|Ill|WD=Q227554|lt=Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞|1=Ill}} Evening Edition, February 23, 1983</ref>.
In 2004, during the hot spring misrepresentation scandal, a ryokan was reported to have displayed descriptions on its website suggesting hot spring usage despite not drawing any; subsequent corrections were made. As of 2019, the management of the affected facility has changed.
== Access == * Railway ({{ill|JR East|ja|JR東日本|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR East|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東日本旅客鉄道|1=East Japan Railway Company|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q499071}}) : Approximately 40 minutes by {{ill|Seibu Sightseeing Bus|ja|西武観光バス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seibu Sightseeing Bus|1=Seibu Kanko Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628934}} from {{ill|Manza-Kazawaguchi Station|en|Manza-Kazawaguchi Station|WD=Q6753421|lt=Manza-Kashizawa-guchi Station|1=Manza-Kazawaguchi Station}} on the {{ill|Agatsuma Line|en|Agatsuma Line|WD=Q391568|lt=Agatsuma Line|1=Agatsuma Line}}. : Approximately 90 minutes by Seibu Sightseeing Bus (via Manza-Kashizawa-guchi Station) from {{ill|Karuizawa Station|en|Karuizawa Station|WD=Q1064215|lt=Karuizawa Station|1=Karuizawa Station}} on the {{ill|Hokuriku Shinkansen|en|Hokuriku Shinkansen|WD=Q1037409|lt=Hokuriku Shinkansen|1=Hokuriku Shinkansen}}. * There are three bus stops in the Manza Onsen area: "Manza Onsen" (near the entrance), the central "Manza Prince Hotel" bus stop, and the "Manza Onsen Ue" bus stop. * Automobile: : The only road available year-round is the paid {{ill|Manza Highway|ja|万座ハイウェー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Highway|1=Manza highway|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353835}}, about 22 km from Mihara in Tsumagoi Village. : From spring to autumn, {{ill|Japan National Route 292|en|Japan National Route 292|WD=Q11421437|lt=National Route 292|1=Japan National Route 292}} (Shiga-Kusatsu Kogen Route) is available (it is closed in winter). Note that due to the eruption alert level for {{ill|Mount Kusatsu-Shirane|en|Mount Kusatsu-Shirane|WD=Q279915|lt=Kusatsu Shirane|1=Mount Kusatsu-Shirane}}, the section between the (Kusatsu) {{ill|Sesshōgahara|ja|殺生河原|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sesshōgahara|1=Sesshōgawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q105410918}} parking area and the Manza Sansaji intersection may be closed. As of April 2019, the eruption alert level is 2; although normally it would be closed all day, an exception allows passage during daytime only (bicycles, motorcycles, and convertibles are not permitted; weather conditions such as fog may also cause closures).

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Manza Onsen]][[ja:万座温泉]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Manza Onsen]][[ja:万座温泉]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|commons:Category:Manza Onsen]][[ja:万座温泉]]
{{Referencesの明記|date=March 2017}}{{infobox Onsen | 名称 = Manza Onsen | native_name = 万座温泉 | image = Manza Onsen Tsumagoi Gunma01bs4272.jpg | コメント = Manza-yama and Manza Onsen | 所在地 = {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture|1=Gunma Prefecture}} {{ill|Agatsuma District, Gunma|en|Agatsuma District, Gunma|WD=Q642394|lt=Agatsuma District|1=Agatsuma District, Gunma}} {{ill|Tsumagoi|en|Tsumagoi|WD=Q1203321|lt=Tsumagoi Village|1=Tsumagoi}} 干俣 | 緯度度 = 36 | 緯度分 = 38 | 緯度秒 = 5.1 | 経度度 = 138 | 経度分 = 30 | 経度秒 = 31.7 | 交通アクセス = ''See the [[#Access|Access]] section.'' | 泉質 = Acidic sulfur spring | 泉温 = Approximately 80°C | 湧出量 = Approximately 5,400,000 {{ill|Litre|en|Litre|WD=Q11582|lt=L|1=Litre}}/day | pH = | 液性の分類 = | 浸透圧の分類 = | 宿泊施設数 = 9 | 総収容人員数 = | 年間浴客数 = | 統計年度 = | External links = http://www.manzaonsen.gr.jp/ | 特記事項 = }}
[[File:Kusatsu-Shiranesan01s5s4272.jpg|thumb|image]] '''Manza Onsen''' (万座温泉) is a hot spring located in Hoshimata, {{ill|Tsumagoi|en|Tsumagoi|WD=Q1203321|lt=Tsumagoi Village|1=Tsumagoi}}, {{ill|Agatsuma District, Gunma|en|Agatsuma District, Gunma|WD=Q642394|lt=Agatsuma District|1=Agatsuma District, Gunma}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture|1=Gunma Prefecture}}, within the {{ill|Jōshin'etsu-kōgen National Park|en|Jōshin'etsu-kōgen National Park|WD=Q1155306|lt=Joshinetsu Kogen National Park|1=Jōshin'etsu-kōgen National Park}}, at an elevation of 1,800 m. It is one of the highest hot springs in Japan that can be reached year-round by private automobile, along with {{ill|Daku River Onsen|ja|濁河温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daku River Onsen|1=Nigorigo Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566108}} in {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|en|Gifu Prefecture|WD=Q131277|lt=Gifu Prefecture|1=Gifu Prefecture}}’s {{ill|Gero, Gifu|en|Gero, Gifu|WD=Q819639|lt=Gero City|1=Gero, Gifu}}. Nearby are three explosive crater lakes: Yubatake, Karagama, and Mizukama.
== Water Quality == * Acidic sulfur spring ({{ill|Hydrogen Sulfide Spring|ja|硫化水素泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hydrogen Sulfide Spring|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 硫黄泉|1=Sulfur spring|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11587679}}) : The water is characterized by its milky white or yellowish appearance and contains the highest sulfur content of any hot spring in Japan. It has a discharge of 3,750 L per minute.
== Onsen Town == [[File:Manza ski resort Mt.Asahi 20080219.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Manza Onsen Ski Resort|ja|万座温泉スキー場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Onsen Ski Resort|1=Manza Onsen Ski Resort|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353840}}]] Located on the slopes of {{ill|Mount Kusatsu-Shirane|en|Mount Kusatsu-Shirane|WD=Q279915|lt=Kusatsu Shirane|1=Mount Kusatsu-Shirane}}, Manza Onsen was once a thriving hot spring resort ({{ill|Yūji|ja|湯治場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yūji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 湯治|1=Thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}}) from spring through autumn and is now renowned as a ski destination in winter. The high temperature of its sources causes a continuous plume of steam, and the source areas display a hellish appearance with a pervasive sulfur odor. Hot spring sources emerge abundantly along the riverbed<ref name="noguchi">{{ill|Ill|en|Ill|WD=Q227554|lt=Fuyuhito Noguchi|ja|野口冬人|1=Ill}}『湯治の宿』, Yomiuri Travel Publishing</ref>. A notable vent known as "Karabuki" near the outskirts of the central area is a local specialty (though its steam output has diminished since around 2012). The gently sloping mountainside is dotted with inns and hotels, with the {{ill|Manza Onsen Ski Resort|ja|万座温泉スキー場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Onsen Ski Resort|1=Manza Onsen Ski Resort|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353840}} situated at the rear. Because the area is within a national park, facility expansions or renovations are limited, resulting in many aging structures. Some accommodations have closed or been absorbed by others; as of 2019, only 7 inns and hotels continue independent operations for individual guests. There are also lodging options with self-catering facilities for long-term hot spring therapy, though no convenience stores exist in the area apart from small shops attached to the accommodations. Additionally, the Manza Onsen Ski Resort was featured as a filming location for the movie [[Take Me Skiing]]. There are over 10 hot spring sources, including Yubatake, Banna no Yu, Daiku Yu, Tetsuyu, and Hōsei no Yu.
== History == The period of initial development is unclear; however, Yayoi pottery has been excavated in the vicinity. Legend has it that {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|en|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|WD=Q941979|lt=Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|1=Sakanoue no Tamuramaro}} exorcised demons here, and records indicate that samurai visited for hot spring therapy during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}. Although it operated as a hot spring resort during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, it did not flourish until after World War II, when the {{ill|Seibu Group|ja|西武グループ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seibu Group|1=Seibu Group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628875}} undertook resort development and transportation improved.
On February 22, 1983, the old building of Manza Onsen Hotel (Nisshinkan) caught fire, completely burning an area of 1,000 m². Four ski guests sustained minor injuries. According to the reports of the {{ill|Naganohara Police Station|ja|長野原警察署|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naganohara Police Station|1=Q11654145|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11654145}} and the {{ill|Agatsuma Western Regional Fire Department|ja|吾妻広域西部消防署|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Agatsuma Western Regional Fire Department|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, although 135 guests were staying in the old building, the deep snow enabled most to escape by jumping from the windows, preventing a far worse tragedy<ref name="mainichi">{{ill|Ill|en|Ill|WD=Q227554|lt=Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞|1=Ill}} Evening Edition, February 23, 1983</ref>.
In 2004, during the hot spring misrepresentation scandal, a ryokan was reported to have displayed descriptions on its website suggesting hot spring usage despite not drawing any; subsequent corrections were made. As of 2019, the management of the affected facility has changed.
== Access == * Railway ({{ill|JR East|ja|JR東日本|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR East|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東日本旅客鉄道|1=East Japan Railway Company|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q499071}}) : Approximately 40 minutes by {{ill|Seibu Sightseeing Bus|ja|西武観光バス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seibu Sightseeing Bus|1=Seibu Kanko Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628934}} from {{ill|Manza-Kazawaguchi Station|en|Manza-Kazawaguchi Station|WD=Q6753421|lt=Manza-Kashizawa-guchi Station|1=Manza-Kazawaguchi Station}} on the {{ill|Agatsuma Line|en|Agatsuma Line|WD=Q391568|lt=Agatsuma Line|1=Agatsuma Line}}. : Approximately 90 minutes by Seibu Sightseeing Bus (via Manza-Kashizawa-guchi Station) from {{ill|Karuizawa Station|en|Karuizawa Station|WD=Q1064215|lt=Karuizawa Station|1=Karuizawa Station}} on the {{ill|Hokuriku Shinkansen|en|Hokuriku Shinkansen|WD=Q1037409|lt=Hokuriku Shinkansen|1=Hokuriku Shinkansen}}. * There are three bus stops in the Manza Onsen area: "Manza Onsen" (near the entrance), the central "Manza Prince Hotel" bus stop, and the "Manza Onsen Ue" bus stop. * Automobile: : The only road available year-round is the paid {{ill|Manza Highway|ja|万座ハイウェー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Highway|1=Manza highway|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353835}}, about 22 km from Mihara in Tsumagoi Village. : From spring to autumn, {{ill|Japan National Route 292|en|Japan National Route 292|WD=Q11421437|lt=National Route 292|1=Japan National Route 292}} (Shiga-Kusatsu Kogen Route) is available (it is closed in winter). Note that due to the eruption alert level for {{ill|Mount Kusatsu-Shirane|en|Mount Kusatsu-Shirane|WD=Q279915|lt=Kusatsu Shirane|1=Mount Kusatsu-Shirane}}, the section between the (Kusatsu) {{ill|Sesshōgahara|ja|殺生河原|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sesshōgahara|1=Sesshōgawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q105410918}} parking area and the Manza Sansaji intersection may be closed. As of April 2019, the eruption alert level is 2; although normally it would be closed all day, an exception allows passage during daytime only (bicycles, motorcycles, and convertibles are not permitted; weather conditions such as fog may also cause closures).

Although it operated as a hot spring resort during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, it did not flourish until after World War II, when the {{ill|Seibu Group|ja|西武グループ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seibu Group|1=Seibu Group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628875}} undertook resort development and transportation improved.
On February 22, 1983, the old building of Manza Onsen Hotel (Nisshinkan) caught fire, completely burning an area of 1,000 m². Four ski guests sustained minor injuries. According to the reports of the {{ill|Naganohara Police Station|ja|長野原警察署|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naganohara Police Station|1=Q11654145|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11654145}} and the {{ill|Agatsuma Western Regional Fire Department|ja|吾妻広域西部消防署|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Agatsuma Western Regional Fire Department|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, although 135 guests were staying in the old building, the deep snow enabled most to escape by jumping from the windows, preventing a far worse tragedy<ref name="mainichi">{{ill|Ill|en|Ill|WD=Q227554|lt=Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞|1=Ill}} Evening Edition, February 23, 1983</ref>.
In 2004, during the hot spring misrepresentation scandal, a ryokan was reported to have displayed descriptions on its website suggesting hot spring usage despite not drawing any; subsequent corrections were made. As of 2019, the management of the affected facility has changed.
== Access == * Railway ({{ill|JR East|ja|JR東日本|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR East|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東日本旅客鉄道|1=East Japan Railway Company|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q499071}}) : Approximately 40 minutes by {{ill|Seibu Sightseeing Bus|ja|西武観光バス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seibu Sightseeing Bus|1=Seibu Kanko Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628934}} from {{ill|Manza-Kazawaguchi Station|en|Manza-Kazawaguchi Station|WD=Q6753421|lt=Manza-Kashizawa-guchi Station|1=Manza-Kazawaguchi Station}} on the {{ill|Agatsuma Line|en|Agatsuma Line|WD=Q391568|lt=Agatsuma Line|1=Agatsuma Line}}. : Approximately 90 minutes by Seibu Sightseeing Bus (via Manza-Kashizawa-guchi Station) from {{ill|Karuizawa Station|en|Karuizawa Station|WD=Q1064215|lt=Karuizawa Station|1=Karuizawa Station}} on the {{ill|Hokuriku Shinkansen|en|Hokuriku Shinkansen|WD=Q1037409|lt=Hokuriku Shinkansen|1=Hokuriku Shinkansen}}. * There are three bus stops in the Manza Onsen area: "Manza Onsen" (near the entrance), the central "Manza Prince Hotel" bus stop, and the "Manza Onsen Ue" bus stop. * Automobile: : The only road available year-round is the paid {{ill|Manza Highway|ja|万座ハイウェー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Highway|1=Manza highway|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353835}}, about 22 km from Mihara in Tsumagoi Village. : From spring to autumn, {{ill|Japan National Route 292|en|Japan National Route 292|WD=Q11421437|lt=National Route 292|1=Japan National Route 292}} (Shiga-Kusatsu Kogen Route) is available (it is closed in winter). Note that due to the eruption alert level for {{ill|Mount Kusatsu-Shirane|en|Mount Kusatsu-Shirane|WD=Q279915|lt=Kusatsu Shirane|1=Mount Kusatsu-Shirane}}, the section between the (Kusatsu) {{ill|Sesshōgahara|ja|殺生河原|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sesshōgahara|1=Sesshōgawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q105410918}} parking area and the Manza Sansaji intersection may be closed. As of April 2019, the eruption alert level is 2; although normally it would be closed all day, an exception allows passage during daytime only (bicycles, motorcycles, and convertibles are not permitted; weather conditions such as fog may also cause closures).

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Manza Onsen]][[ja:万座温泉]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Manza Onsen]][[ja:万座温泉]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:万座温泉]]
{{Referencesの明記|date=March 2017}}{{infobox Onsen | 名称 = Manza Onsen | native_name = 万座温泉 | image = Manza Onsen Tsumagoi Gunma01bs4272.jpg | コメント = Manza-yama and Manza Onsen | 所在地 = {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture|1=Gunma Prefecture}} {{ill|Agatsuma District, Gunma|en|Agatsuma District, Gunma|WD=Q642394|lt=Agatsuma District|1=Agatsuma District, Gunma}} {{ill|Tsumagoi|en|Tsumagoi|WD=Q1203321|lt=Tsumagoi Village|1=Tsumagoi}} 干俣 | 緯度度 = 36 | 緯度分 = 38 | 緯度秒 = 5.1 | 経度度 = 138 | 経度分 = 30 | 経度秒 = 31.7 | 交通アクセス = ''See the [[#Access|Access]] section.'' | 泉質 = Acidic sulfur spring | 泉温 = Approximately 80°C | 湧出量 = Approximately 5,400,000 {{ill|Litre|en|Litre|WD=Q11582|lt=L|1=Litre}}/day | pH = | 液性の分類 = | 浸透圧の分類 = | 宿泊施設数 = 9 | 総収容人員数 = | 年間浴客数 = | 統計年度 = | External links = http://www.manzaonsen.gr.jp/ | 特記事項 = }}
[[File:Kusatsu-Shiranesan01s5s4272.jpg|thumb|image]] '''Manza Onsen''' (万座温泉) is a hot spring located in Hoshimata, {{ill|Tsumagoi|en|Tsumagoi|WD=Q1203321|lt=Tsumagoi Village|1=Tsumagoi}}, {{ill|Agatsuma District, Gunma|en|Agatsuma District, Gunma|WD=Q642394|lt=Agatsuma District|1=Agatsuma District, Gunma}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture|1=Gunma Prefecture}}, within the {{ill|Jōshin'etsu-kōgen National Park|en|Jōshin'etsu-kōgen National Park|WD=Q1155306|lt=Joshinetsu Kogen National Park|1=Jōshin'etsu-kōgen National Park}}, at an elevation of 1,800 m. It is one of the highest hot springs in Japan that can be reached year-round by private automobile, along with {{ill|Daku River Onsen|ja|濁河温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daku River Onsen|1=Nigorigo Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566108}} in {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|en|Gifu Prefecture|WD=Q131277|lt=Gifu Prefecture|1=Gifu Prefecture}}’s {{ill|Gero, Gifu|en|Gero, Gifu|WD=Q819639|lt=Gero City|1=Gero, Gifu}}. Nearby are three explosive crater lakes: Yubatake, Karagama, and Mizukama.
== Water Quality == * Acidic sulfur spring ({{ill|Hydrogen Sulfide Spring|ja|硫化水素泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hydrogen Sulfide Spring|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 硫黄泉|1=Sulfur spring|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11587679}}) : The water is characterized by its milky white or yellowish appearance and contains the highest sulfur content of any hot spring in Japan. It has a discharge of 3,750 L per minute.
== Onsen Town == [[File:Manza ski resort Mt.Asahi 20080219.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Manza Onsen Ski Resort|ja|万座温泉スキー場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Onsen Ski Resort|1=Manza Onsen Ski Resort|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353840}}]] Located on the slopes of {{ill|Mount Kusatsu-Shirane|en|Mount Kusatsu-Shirane|WD=Q279915|lt=Kusatsu Shirane|1=Mount Kusatsu-Shirane}}, Manza Onsen was once a thriving hot spring resort ({{ill|Yūji|ja|湯治場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yūji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 湯治|1=Thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}}) from spring through autumn and is now renowned as a ski destination in winter. The high temperature of its sources causes a continuous plume of steam, and the source areas display a hellish appearance with a pervasive sulfur odor. Hot spring sources emerge abundantly along the riverbed<ref name="noguchi">{{ill|Ill|en|Ill|WD=Q227554|lt=Fuyuhito Noguchi|ja|野口冬人|1=Ill}}『湯治の宿』, Yomiuri Travel Publishing</ref>. A notable vent known as "Karabuki" near the outskirts of the central area is a local specialty (though its steam output has diminished since around 2012). The gently sloping mountainside is dotted with inns and hotels, with the {{ill|Manza Onsen Ski Resort|ja|万座温泉スキー場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Onsen Ski Resort|1=Manza Onsen Ski Resort|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353840}} situated at the rear. Because the area is within a national park, facility expansions or renovations are limited, resulting in many aging structures. Some accommodations have closed or been absorbed by others; as of 2019, only 7 inns and hotels continue independent operations for individual guests. There are also lodging options with self-catering facilities for long-term hot spring therapy, though no convenience stores exist in the area apart from small shops attached to the accommodations. Additionally, the Manza Onsen Ski Resort was featured as a filming location for the movie [[Take Me Skiing]]. There are over 10 hot spring sources, including Yubatake, Banna no Yu, Daiku Yu, Tetsuyu, and Hōsei no Yu.
== History == The period of initial development is unclear; however, Yayoi pottery has been excavated in the vicinity. Legend has it that {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|en|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|WD=Q941979|lt=Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|1=Sakanoue no Tamuramaro}} exorcised demons here, and records indicate that samurai visited for hot spring therapy during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}. Although it operated as a hot spring resort during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, it did not flourish until after World War II, when the {{ill|Seibu Group|ja|西武グループ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seibu Group|1=Seibu Group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628875}} undertook resort development and transportation improved.
On February 22, 1983, the old building of Manza Onsen Hotel (Nisshinkan) caught fire, completely burning an area of 1,000 m². Four ski guests sustained minor injuries. According to the reports of the {{ill|Naganohara Police Station|ja|長野原警察署|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naganohara Police Station|1=Q11654145|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11654145}} and the {{ill|Agatsuma Western Regional Fire Department|ja|吾妻広域西部消防署|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Agatsuma Western Regional Fire Department|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, although 135 guests were staying in the old building, the deep snow enabled most to escape by jumping from the windows, preventing a far worse tragedy<ref name="mainichi">{{ill|Ill|en|Ill|WD=Q227554|lt=Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞|1=Ill}} Evening Edition, February 23, 1983</ref>.
In 2004, during the hot spring misrepresentation scandal, a ryokan was reported to have displayed descriptions on its website suggesting hot spring usage despite not drawing any; subsequent corrections were made. As of 2019, the management of the affected facility has changed.
== Access == * Railway ({{ill|JR East|ja|JR東日本|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR East|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東日本旅客鉄道|1=East Japan Railway Company|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q499071}}) : Approximately 40 minutes by {{ill|Seibu Sightseeing Bus|ja|西武観光バス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seibu Sightseeing Bus|1=Seibu Kanko Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628934}} from {{ill|Manza-Kazawaguchi Station|en|Manza-Kazawaguchi Station|WD=Q6753421|lt=Manza-Kashizawa-guchi Station|1=Manza-Kazawaguchi Station}} on the {{ill|Agatsuma Line|en|Agatsuma Line|WD=Q391568|lt=Agatsuma Line|1=Agatsuma Line}}. : Approximately 90 minutes by Seibu Sightseeing Bus (via Manza-Kashizawa-guchi Station) from {{ill|Karuizawa Station|en|Karuizawa Station|WD=Q1064215|lt=Karuizawa Station|1=Karuizawa Station}} on the {{ill|Hokuriku Shinkansen|en|Hokuriku Shinkansen|WD=Q1037409|lt=Hokuriku Shinkansen|1=Hokuriku Shinkansen}}. * There are three bus stops in the Manza Onsen area: "Manza Onsen" (near the entrance), the central "Manza Prince Hotel" bus stop, and the "Manza Onsen Ue" bus stop. * Automobile: : The only road available year-round is the paid {{ill|Manza Highway|ja|万座ハイウェー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Highway|1=Manza highway|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353835}}, about 22 km from Mihara in Tsumagoi Village. : From spring to autumn, {{ill|Japan National Route 292|en|Japan National Route 292|WD=Q11421437|lt=National Route 292|1=Japan National Route 292}} (Shiga-Kusatsu Kogen Route) is available (it is closed in winter). Note that due to the eruption alert level for {{ill|Mount Kusatsu-Shirane|en|Mount Kusatsu-Shirane|WD=Q279915|lt=Kusatsu Shirane|1=Mount Kusatsu-Shirane}}, the section between the (Kusatsu) {{ill|Sesshōgahara|ja|殺生河原|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sesshōgahara|1=Sesshōgawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q105410918}} parking area and the Manza Sansaji intersection may be closed. As of April 2019, the eruption alert level is 2; although normally it would be closed all day, an exception allows passage during daytime only (bicycles, motorcycles, and convertibles are not permitted; weather conditions such as fog may also cause closures).

Although it operated as a hot spring resort during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, it did not flourish until after World War II, when the {{ill|Seibu Group|ja|西武グループ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seibu Group|1=Seibu Group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11628875}} undertook resort development and transportation improved.
On February 22, 1983, the old building of Manza Onsen Hotel (Nisshinkan) caught fire, completely burning an area of 1,000 m². Four ski guests sustained minor injuries. According to the reports of the {{ill|Naganohara Police Station|ja|長野原警察署|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naganohara Police Station|1=Q11654145|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11654145}} and the {{ill|Agatsuma Western Regional Fire Department|ja|吾妻広域西部消防署|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Agatsuma Western Regional Fire Department|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, although 135 guests were staying in the old building, the deep snow enabled most to escape by jumping from the windows, preventing a far worse tragedy<ref name="mainichi">{{ill|Ill|en|Ill|qid=Q227554|lt=Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞|1=Ill}} Evening Edition, February 23, 1983</ref>.
In 2004, during the hot spring misrepresentation scandal, a ryokan was reported to have displayed descriptions on its website suggesting hot spring usage despite not drawing any; subsequent corrections were made. As of 2019, the management of the affected facility has changed.
== Access == * Railway ({{ill|JR East|ja|JR東日本|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR East|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東日本旅客鉄道|1=East Japan Railway Company|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q499071}}) : Approximately 40 minutes by {{ill|Seibu Sightseeing Bus|ja|西武観光バス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seibu Sightseeing Bus|1=Seibu Kanko Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11628934}} from {{ill|Manza-Kazawaguchi Station|en|Manza-Kazawaguchi Station|qid=Q6753421|lt=Manza-Kashizawa-guchi Station|1=Manza-Kazawaguchi Station}} on the {{ill|Agatsuma Line|en|Agatsuma Line|qid=Q391568|lt=Agatsuma Line|1=Agatsuma Line}}. : Approximately 90 minutes by Seibu Sightseeing Bus (via Manza-Kashizawa-guchi Station) from {{ill|Karuizawa Station|en|Karuizawa Station|qid=Q1064215|lt=Karuizawa Station|1=Karuizawa Station}} on the {{ill|Hokuriku Shinkansen|en|Hokuriku Shinkansen|qid=Q1037409|lt=Hokuriku Shinkansen|1=Hokuriku Shinkansen}}. * There are three bus stops in the Manza Onsen area: "Manza Onsen" (near the entrance), the central "Manza Prince Hotel" bus stop, and the "Manza Onsen Ue" bus stop. * Automobile: : The only road available year-round is the paid {{ill|Manza Highway|ja|万座ハイウェー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Highway|1=Manza highway|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11353835}}, about 22 km from Mihara in Tsumagoi Village. : From spring to autumn, {{ill|Japan National Route 292|en|Japan National Route 292|qid=Q11421437|lt=National Route 292|1=Japan National Route 292}} (Shiga-Kusatsu Kogen Route) is available (it is closed in winter). Note that due to the eruption alert level for {{ill|Mount Kusatsu-Shirane|en|Mount Kusatsu-Shirane|qid=Q279915|lt=Kusatsu Shirane|1=Mount Kusatsu-Shirane}}, the section between the (Kusatsu) {{ill|Sesshōgahara|ja|殺生河原|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sesshōgahara|1=Sesshōgawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q105410918}} parking area and the Manza Sansaji intersection may be closed. As of April 2019, the eruption alert level is 2; although normally it would be closed all day, an exception allows passage during daytime only (bicycles, motorcycles, and convertibles are not permitted; weather conditions such as fog may also cause closures).
